revision.comment: /* 概要 */新規作成
revision.text:
{{基礎情報 日本の神 | 名 = 宇都志日金拆命 | 世代名 = | 先代= | 次代= | 神祇 = <!-- 天神、地祇、天津神、国津神など -->[[国津神]] | 全名 = 宇都志日金拆命 | 別名 = 宇都志日金折命、顕日金拆尊、穂高見命 | 別称 = | 神階 = [[正五位]]下 | 神格 = <!-- 太陽、月、山、海など司るもの -->[[海神]]、開拓神 | 陵所 = | 父 = [[綿津見神|綿津見豊玉毘古命]]（綿津見三神） | 母 = | 親 = <!-- まぐあいによって生まれない場合 --> | 配偶者 = | 子 = [[多久置命]] | 宮 = | 神社 = [[穂高神社]] 等 | 関連氏族 = [[阿曇氏]]、[[阿曇犬養氏]]、[[海犬養氏]]、[[和邇氏]]、[[倭国造]]、[[小野氏]]、[[八木氏]]、[[尾張氏]]、[[額田国造]]、[[近淡海国造]] }} '''宇都志日金拆命'''（うつしひかなさくのみこと）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[神 (神道)|神]]。
== 概要 == 『[[古事記]]』では'''宇都志日金拆命'''と表記し、その他の史料では'''穂高見命'''（ほだかみのみこと）の名で伝わる。
『古事記』に登場する海の[[国津神]]で、[[伊邪那岐命]]が[[黄泉国]]から帰ってきた後、日向の瀬で禊を行った際に生まれた[[綿津見神]]たちの子で、[[阿曇氏]]の祖神と伝わる。
== 系譜 == [[綿津見神]]の子で、子神に[[多久置命]]がいる。
== 祀る神社 == == 脚注 == {{Reflist}} {{Notelist}}
== 関連項目 == *[[海人族]] *[[海人]]
{{デフォルトソート:うつしひかなさくのみこと}} [[Category:日本の神]] [[Category:国津神]]

revision.comment: /* 祀る神社 */修正
revision.text:
{{基礎情報 日本の神 | 名 = 宇都志日金拆命 | 世代名 = | 先代= | 次代= | 神祇 = <!-- 天神、地祇、天津神、国津神など -->[[国津神]] | 全名 = 宇都志日金拆命 | 別名 = 宇都志日金折命、顕日金拆尊、穂高見命 | 別称 = | 神階 = [[正五位]]下 | 神格 = <!-- 太陽、月、山、海など司るもの -->[[海神]]、開拓神 | 陵所 = | 父 = [[綿津見神|綿津見豊玉毘古命]]（綿津見三神） | 母 = | 親 = <!-- まぐあいによって生まれない場合 --> | 配偶者 = | 子 = [[多久置命]] | 宮 = | 神社 = [[穂高神社]] 等 | 関連氏族 = [[阿曇氏]]、[[阿曇犬養氏]]、[[海犬養氏]]、[[和邇氏]]、[[倭国造]]、[[小野氏]]、[[八木氏]]、[[尾張氏]]、[[額田国造]]、[[近淡海国造]] }} '''宇都志日金拆命'''（うつしひかなさくのみこと）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[神 (神道)|神]]。
== 概要 == 『[[古事記]]』では'''宇都志日金拆命'''と表記し、その他の史料では'''穂高見命'''（ほだかみのみこと）の名で伝わる。
『古事記』に登場する海の[[国津神]]で、[[伊邪那岐命]]が[[黄泉国]]から帰ってきた後、日向の瀬で禊を行った際に生まれた[[綿津見神]]たちの子で、[[阿曇氏]]の祖神と伝わる。
== 系譜 == [[綿津見神]]の子で、子神に[[多久置命]]がいる。
== 祀る神社 == *[[穂高神社]] 本宮（[[長野県]][[安曇野市]]穂高） *穂高神社 奥宮（長野県[[松本市]]安曇上高地） *[[氷銫斗賣神社]]（長野県[[長野市]][[稲里町]]） *[[出水神社]]（[[石川県]][[加賀市]][[橋立町]]） *[[須賀神社 (沼田市)|須賀神社]]（[[群馬県]][[沼田市]]中町） *[[金拆神社]]（[[奈良県]][[桜井市]]大字三輪） *[[海神社 (紀の川市)|海神社]] 境内摂社（[[和歌山県]][[紀の川市]]神領） *[[胡簶神社]]（[[長崎県]][[対馬市]][[上対馬町]]）
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}} {{Notelist}}
== 関連項目 == *[[海人族]] *[[海人]]
{{デフォルトソート:うつしひかなさくのみこと}} [[Category:日本の神]] [[Category:国津神]]

revision.text:
{{基礎情報 日本の神 | 名 = 宇都志日金拆命 | 世代名 = | 先代= | 次代= | 神祇 = <!-- 天神、地祇、天津神、国津神など -->[[国津神]] | 全名 = 宇都志日金拆命 | 別名 = 宇都志日金析命、宇都志日金柝命、宇都志日金折命、顕日金拆尊、穂高見命 | 別称 = | 神階 = | 神格 = <!-- 太陽、月、山、海など司るもの -->[[海神]]、漁労神、開拓神 | 陵所 = | 父 = [[綿津見神|綿積豊玉彦神]]（綿津見三神） | 母 = | 親 = <!-- まぐあいによって生まれない場合 --> | 配偶者 = | 子 = [[多久置命]] | 宮 = | 神社 = [[穂高神社]] 等 | 関連氏族 = [[阿曇氏]]、[[阿曇犬養氏]]、[[海犬養氏]]、[[和邇氏]]、[[小野氏]]、[[額田国造]]、[[近淡海国造]] }} '''宇都志日金拆命'''（うつしひかなさくのみこと）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[神 (神道)|神]]。
== 概要 == 『[[古事記]]』では'''宇都志日金拆命'''と表記し、その他の史料では'''穂高見命'''（ほだかみのみこと）の名で伝わる。
『古事記』に登場する海の[[国津神]]で、[[伊邪那岐命]]が[[黄泉国]]から帰ってきた後、日向の瀬で禊を行った際に生まれた[[綿津見神]]たちの子で、[[阿曇氏]]の祖神と伝わる。
== 系譜 == [[綿津見神]]の子で、子神に[[多久置命]]がいる。
== 後裔 == *[[阿曇氏]] *[[阿曇犬養氏]] *[[海犬養氏]] *[[和邇氏]] *[[小野氏]] *[[額田国造]] *[[近淡海国造]]
== 祀る神社 == *[[穂高神社]] 本宮（[[長野県]][[安曇野市]]穂高） *穂高神社 奥宮（長野県[[松本市]]安曇上高地） *[[氷鉋斗賣神社]]（長野県[[長野市]][[稲里町 (長野市)|稲里町]]） *[[出水神社]]（[[石川県]][[加賀市]][[橋立町]]） *[[須賀神社 (沼田市)|須賀神社]]（[[群馬県]][[沼田市]]中町） *[[金拆神社]]（[[奈良県]][[桜井市]]大字三輪） *[[海神社 (紀の川市)|海神社]] 境内摂社（[[和歌山県]][[紀の川市]]神領） *[[志賀海神社]] 摂社 今宮神社（[[福岡県]][[福岡市]][[東区 (福岡市)|東区]]） *[[胡簶神社]]（[[長崎県]][[対馬市]][[上対馬町]]）
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}} {{Notelist}}
== 関連項目 == *[[海人族]] *[[海人]]

revision.text:
{{基礎情報 日本の神 | 名 = 宇都志日金拆命 | 世代名 = | 先代= | 次代= | 神祇 = <!-- 天神、地祇、天津神、国津神など -->[[国津神]] | 全名 = 宇都志日金拆命 | 別名 = 宇都志日金析命、宇都志日金柝命、宇都志日金折命、顕日金拆尊、穂高見命 | 別称 = | 神階 = | 神格 = <!-- 太陽、月、山、海など司るもの -->[[海神]]、漁労神、開拓神 | 陵所 = | 父 = [[綿津見神|綿積豊玉彦神]]（綿津見三神） | 母 = | 親 = <!-- まぐあいによって生まれない場合 --> | 配偶者 = | 子 = [[多久置命]] | 宮 = | 神社 = [[穂高神社]] 等 | 関連氏族 = [[阿曇氏]]、[[阿曇犬養氏]]、[[海犬養氏]]、[[和邇氏]]、[[小野氏]]、[[額田国造]]、[[近淡海国造]] }} '''宇都志日金拆命'''（うつしひかなさくのみこと）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[神 (神道)|神]]。
== 概要 == 『[[古事記]]』では'''宇都志日金拆命'''と表記し、その他の史料では'''穂高見命'''（ほだかみのみこと）の名で伝わる。
『古事記』に登場する海の[[国津神]]で、[[伊邪那岐命]]が[[黄泉国]]から帰ってきた後、日向の瀬で禊を行った際に生まれた[[綿津見神]]たちの子で、[[阿曇氏]]の祖神と伝わる。
[[佐久郡]]の地名由来として、宇都志日金拆命が開拓したので「拆 サク」となったという説がある<ref>『佐久新聞』佐久史考小林基茂昭和59年1月30日</ref>。
また[[穂高岳]]に降臨し、[[信濃国]]の安曇氏の祖となったともされる。
== 系譜 == [[綿津見神]]の子で、子神に[[多久置命]]がいる。
== 後裔 == *[[阿曇氏]] *[[阿曇犬養氏]] *[[海犬養氏]] *[[和邇氏]] *[[小野氏]] *[[額田国造]] *[[近淡海国造]]
== 祀る神社 == *[[穂高神社]] 本宮（[[長野県]][[安曇野市]]穂高） *穂高神社 奥宮（長野県[[松本市]]安曇上高地） *[[氷鉋斗賣神社]]（長野県[[長野市]][[稲里町 (長野市)|稲里町]]） *[[出水神社]]（[[石川県]][[加賀市]][[橋立町]]） *[[須賀神社 (沼田市)|須賀神社]]（[[群馬県]][[沼田市]]中町） *[[金拆神社]]（[[奈良県]][[桜井市]]大字三輪） *[[海神社 (紀の川市)|海神社]] 境内摂社（[[和歌山県]][[紀の川市]]神領） *[[志賀海神社]] 摂社 今宮神社（[[福岡県]][[福岡市]][[東区 (福岡市)|東区]]） *[[胡簶神社]]（[[長崎県]][[対馬市]][[上対馬町]]）

== 関連項目 == *[[海人族]] *[[海人]]

revision.comment: /* 系譜 */修正
revision.text:
{{基礎情報 日本の神 | 名 = 宇都志日金拆命 | 世代名 = | 先代= | 次代= | 神祇 = <!-- 天神、地祇、天津神、国津神など -->[[国津神]] | 全名 = 宇都志日金拆命 | 別名 = 宇都志日金析命、宇都志日金柝命、宇都志日金折命、顕日金拆尊、穂高見命 | 別称 = | 神階 = | 神格 = <!-- 太陽、月、山、海など司るもの -->[[海神]]、漁労神、開拓神 | 陵所 = | 父 = [[綿津見神|綿積豊玉彦神]]（綿津見三神） | 母 = | 親 = <!-- まぐあいによって生まれない場合 --> | 配偶者 = | 子 = [[多久置命]] | 宮 = | 神社 = [[穂高神社]] 等 | 関連氏族 = [[阿曇氏]]、[[阿曇犬養氏]]、[[海犬養氏]]、[[和邇氏]]、[[小野氏]]、[[額田国造]]、[[近淡海国造]] }} '''宇都志日金拆命'''（うつしひかなさくのみこと）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[神 (神道)|神]]。
== 概要 == 『[[古事記]]』では'''宇都志日金拆命'''と表記し、その他の史料では'''穂高見命'''（ほだかみのみこと）の名で伝わる。
『古事記』に登場する海の[[国津神]]で、[[伊邪那岐命]]が[[黄泉国]]から帰ってきた後、日向の瀬で禊を行った際に生まれた[[綿津見神]]たちの子で、[[阿曇氏]]の祖神と伝わる。
[[佐久郡]]の地名由来として、宇都志日金拆命が開拓したので「拆 サク」となったという説がある<ref>『佐久新聞』佐久史考小林基茂昭和59年1月30日</ref>。
また[[穂高岳]]に降臨し、[[信濃国]]の安曇氏の祖となったともされる。
== 系譜 == [[綿津見神]]の子で、子神に安曇氏の祖神である[[多久置命]]がいる。また[[尾張氏]]、[[八木氏]]、[[倭国造]]などの祖である[[布留多摩命]]や[[火遠理命]]の后である[[豊玉毘売命]]、[[神武天皇]]の母である[[タマヨリビメ (日向神話)|玉依毘売命]]が兄弟姉妹とされる。ただし他氏族の世代関係から豊玉毘売命と玉依毘売命が実際には宇都志日金拆命の子に位置づけられると考えられる<ref>[[宝賀寿男]]「[http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/kodaisi/yamasho/ookuni1.htm 天照大神と大国主神の関係─筑紫国と高天原神話・日向三代神話─]」『古樹紀之房間』、2017年。</ref>。
== 後裔 == *[[阿曇氏]] *[[阿曇犬養氏]] *[[海犬養氏]] *[[和邇氏]] *[[小野氏]] *[[額田国造]] *[[近淡海国造]]
== 祀る神社 == *[[穂高神社]] 本宮（[[長野県]][[安曇野市]]穂高） *穂高神社 奥宮（長野県[[松本市]]安曇上高地） *[[氷鉋斗賣神社]]（長野県[[長野市]][[稲里町 (長野市)|稲里町]]） *[[出水神社]]（[[石川県]][[加賀市]][[橋立町]]） *[[須賀神社 (沼田市)|須賀神社]]（[[群馬県]][[沼田市]]中町） *[[金拆神社]]（[[奈良県]][[桜井市]]大字三輪） *[[海神社 (紀の川市)|海神社]] 境内摂社（[[和歌山県]][[紀の川市]]神領） *[[志賀海神社]] 摂社 今宮神社（[[福岡県]][[福岡市]][[東区 (福岡市)|東区]]） *[[胡簶神社]]（[[長崎県]][[対馬市]][[上対馬町]]）
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}} {{Notelist}}
== 関連項目 == *[[海人族]] *[[海人]]
{{デフォルトソート:うつしひかなさくのみこと}} [[Category:日本の神]] [[Category:国津神]]

{{基礎情報 日本の神 | 名 = 宇都志日金拆命 | 世代名 = | 先代= | 次代= | 神祇 = <!-- 天神、地祇、天津神、国津神など -->[[国津神]] | 全名 = 宇都志日金拆命 | 別名 = 宇都志日金析命、宇都志日金柝命、宇都志日金折命、顕日金拆尊、穂高見命 | 別称 = | 神階 = | 神格 = <!-- 太陽、月、山、海など司るもの -->[[海神]]、漁労神、開拓神 | 陵所 = | 父 = [[綿津見神|綿積豊玉彦神]]（綿津見三神） | 母 = | 親 = <!-- まぐあいによって生まれない場合 --> | 配偶者 = | 子 = [[多久置命]] | 宮 = | 神社 = [[穂高神社]] 等 | 関連氏族 = [[安曇氏]]、[[安曇犬養氏]]、[[海犬養氏]] }} '''宇都志日金拆命'''（うつしひかなさくのみこと）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[神 (神道)|神]]。
== 概要 == 『[[古事記]]』では'''宇都志日金拆命'''と表記し、その他の史料では'''穂高見命'''（ほだかみのみこと）の名で伝わる。
『古事記』に登場する海の[[国津神]]で、[[伊邪那岐命]]が[[黄泉国]]から帰ってきた後、日向の瀬で禊を行った際に生まれた[[綿津見神]]たちの子で、[[阿曇氏|安曇氏]]の祖神と伝わる。
[[佐久郡]]の地名由来として、宇都志日金拆命が開拓したので「拆 サク」となったという説がある<ref>『佐久新聞』佐久史考小林基茂昭和59年1月30日</ref>。
また[[穂高岳]]に降臨し、[[信濃国]]の安曇氏の祖となったともされる。
== 系譜 == [[綿津見神]]の子で、子神に安曇氏の祖神である[[多久置命]]がいる。また[[尾張氏]]、[[八木氏]]、[[倭国造]]などの祖である[[布留多摩命]]や[[火遠理命]]の后である[[豊玉毘売命]]、[[神武天皇]]の母である[[タマヨリビメ (日向神話)|玉依毘売命]]が兄弟姉妹とされる。
宝賀寿男は、他氏族の世代関係から豊玉毘売命と玉依毘売命が実際には宇都志日金拆命の子に位置づけられると主張した<ref>[[宝賀寿男]]「[http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/kodaisi/yamasho/ookuni1.htm 天照大神と大国主神の関係─筑紫国と高天原神話・日向三代神話─]」『古樹紀之房間』、2017年。</ref>。
== 後裔 == *[[阿曇氏|安曇氏]] *[[阿曇犬養氏|安曇犬養氏]] *[[海犬養氏]] *[[和邇氏]] *[[小野氏]] *[[額田国造]] *[[近淡海国造]]
== 祀る神社 == *[[穂高神社]] 本宮（[[長野県]][[安曇野市]]穂高） *穂高神社 奥宮（長野県[[松本市]]安曇上高地） *[[氷鉋斗賣神社]]（長野県[[長野市]][[稲里町 (長野市)|稲里町]]） *[[出水神社]]（[[石川県]][[加賀市]][[橋立町]]） *[[須賀神社 (沼田市)|須賀神社]]（[[群馬県]][[沼田市]]中町） *[[金拆神社]]（[[奈良県]][[桜井市]]大字三輪） *[[海神社 (紀の川市)|海神社]] 境内摂社（[[和歌山県]][[紀の川市]]神領） *[[志賀海神社]] 摂社 今宮神社（[[福岡県]][[福岡市]][[東区 (福岡市)|東区]]） *[[胡簶神社]]（[[長崎県]][[対馬市]][[上対馬町]]）
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}} {{Notelist}}
== 関連項目 == *[[海人族]] *[[海人]]
{{デフォルトソート:うつしひかなさくのみこと}} [[Category:日本の神]] [[Category:国津神]]

{{基礎情報 日本の神 | 名 = 宇都志日金拆命 | 世代名 = | 先代= | 次代= | 神祇 = <!-- 天神、地祇、天津神、国津神など -->[[国津神]] | 全名 = 宇都志日金拆命 | 別名 = 宇都志日金析命、宇都志日金柝命、宇都志日金折命、顕日金拆尊、穂高見命 | 別称 = | 神階 = | 神格 = <!-- 太陽、月、山、海など司るもの -->[[海神]]、漁労神、開拓神 | 陵所 = | 父 = [[綿津見神|綿積豊玉彦神]]（綿津見三神） | 母 = | 親 = <!-- まぐあいによって生まれない場合 --> | 配偶者 = | 子 = | 宮 = | 神社 = [[穂高神社]] 等 | 関連氏族 = [[安曇氏]]、[[安曇犬養氏]]、[[海犬養氏]] }} '''宇都志日金拆命'''（うつしひかなさくのみこと）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[神 (神道)|神]]。
== 概要 == 『[[古事記]]』では'''宇都志日金拆命'''と表記し、その他の史料では'''穂高見命'''（ほだかみのみこと）の名で伝わる。
『古事記』に登場する海の[[国津神]]で、[[伊邪那岐命]]が[[黄泉国]]から帰ってきた後、日向の瀬で禊を行った際に生まれた[[綿津見神]]たちの子で、[[阿曇氏|安曇氏]]の祖神と伝わる。
[[佐久郡]]の地名由来として、宇都志日金拆命が開拓したので「拆（サク）」となったという説がある<ref>『佐久新聞』佐久史考小林基茂昭和59年1月30日</ref>。
また、[[穂高岳]]に降臨し、[[信濃国]]の安曇氏の祖となったともされる{{要出典|date=2022年4月}}。
== 系譜 == [[火遠理命]]の后である[[豊玉毘売命]]、[[神武天皇]]の母である[[タマヨリビメ (日向神話)|玉依毘売命]]が兄弟姉妹とされる。
また、安曇氏や海犬養氏などの祖神であるという<ref>佐藤雄一「古代信濃の氏族と信仰」（2021年、吉川弘文館）</ref>。
== 後裔 == *[[阿曇氏|安曇氏]] *[[阿曇犬養氏|安曇犬養氏]] *[[海犬養氏]]
== 祀る神社 == *[[穂高神社]] 本宮（[[長野県]][[安曇野市]]穂高） *穂高神社 奥宮（長野県[[松本市]]安曇上高地） *[[氷鉋斗賣神社]]（長野県[[長野市]][[稲里町 (長野市)|稲里町]]） *[[出水神社]]（[[石川県]][[加賀市]][[橋立町]]） *[[須賀神社 (沼田市)|須賀神社]]（[[群馬県]][[沼田市]]中町） *[[金拆神社]]（[[奈良県]][[桜井市]]大字三輪） *[[海神社 (紀の川市)|海神社]] 境内摂社（[[和歌山県]][[紀の川市]]神領） *[[志賀海神社]] 摂社 今宮神社（[[福岡県]][[福岡市]][[東区 (福岡市)|東区]]） *[[胡簶神社]]（[[長崎県]][[対馬市]][[上対馬町]]）
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}} {{Notelist}}
== 関連項目 == *[[海人族]] *[[海人]]
{{デフォルトソート:うつしひかなさくのみこと}} [[Category:日本の神]] [[Category:国津神]]

== 概要 == 『[[古事記]]』では'''宇都志日金拆命'''と表記し、その他の史料では'''穂高見命'''（ほだかみのみこと）の名で伝わる。

また、[[穂高岳]]に降臨し、[[信濃国]]の安曇氏の祖となったともされる{{要出典|date=2022年4月}}。

また、安曇氏や海犬養氏などの祖神であるという<ref>佐藤雄一「古代信濃の氏族と信仰」（2021年、吉川弘文館）</ref>。

== 祀る神社 == *[[穂高神社]] 本宮（[[長野県]][[安曇野市]]穂高） *穂高神社 奥宮（長野県[[松本市]]安曇上高地） *[[氷鉋斗賣神社]]（長野県[[長野市]][[稲里町 (長野市)|稲里町]]） *[[出水神社]]（[[石川県]][[加賀市]][[橋立町]]） *[[須賀神社 (沼田市)|須賀神社]]（[[群馬県]][[沼田市]]中町） *[[金拆神社]]（[[奈良県]][[桜井市]]大字三輪） *[[海神社 (紀の川市)|海神社]] 境内摂社（[[和歌山県]][[紀の川市]]神領） *[[志賀海神社]] 摂社 今宮神社（[[福岡県]][[福岡市]][[東区 (福岡市)|東区]]） *[[胡簶神社]]（[[長崎県]][[対馬市]][[上対馬町]]）

revision.text:
{{基礎情報 日本の神 | 名 = 宇都志日金拆命 | 世代名 = | 先代= | 次代= | 神祇 = <!-- 天神、地祇、天津神、国津神など -->[[国津神]] | 全名 = 宇都志日金拆命 | 別名 = 宇都志日金析命、宇都志日金柝命、宇都志日金折命、顕日金拆尊、穂高見命 | 別称 = | 神階 = | 神格 = <!-- 太陽、月、山、海など司るもの -->[[海神]]、漁労神、開拓神 | 陵所 = | 父 = [[綿津見神|綿積豊玉彦神]]（綿津見三神） | 母 = | 親 = <!-- まぐあいによって生まれない場合 --> | 配偶者 = | 子 = | 宮 = | 神社 = [[穂高神社]] 等 | 関連氏族 = [[安曇氏]]、[[安曇犬養氏]]、[[海犬養氏]] }} '''宇都志日金拆命'''（うつしひかなさくのみこと）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[神 (神道)|神]]。
== 概要 == 『[[古事記]]』では'''宇都志日金拆命'''と表記し、その他の史料では'''穂高見命'''（ほだかみのみこと）の名で伝わる。
『古事記』に登場する海の[[国津神]]で、[[伊邪那岐命]]が[[黄泉国]]から帰ってきた後、日向の瀬で禊を行った際に生まれた[[綿津見神]]たちの子で、[[阿曇氏|安曇氏]]の祖神と伝わる。
[[佐久郡]]の地名由来として、宇都志日金拆命が開拓したので「拆（サク）」となったという説がある<ref>『佐久新聞』佐久史考小林基茂昭和59年1月30日</ref>。
また、[[穂高岳]]に降臨し、[[信濃国]]の安曇氏の祖となったともされる{{要出典|date=2022年4月}}。
== 系譜 == [[火遠理命]]の后である[[豊玉毘売命]]、[[神武天皇]]の母である[[タマヨリビメ (日向神話)|玉依毘売命]]が兄弟姉妹とされる。
また、安曇氏や海犬養氏などの祖神であるという<ref>佐藤雄一「古代信濃の氏族と信仰」（2021年、吉川弘文館）</ref>。
== 後裔 == *[[阿曇氏|安曇氏]] *[[阿曇犬養氏|安曇犬養氏]] *[[海犬養氏]]
== 祀る神社 == *[[穂高神社]] 本宮（[[長野県]][[安曇野市]]穂高） *穂高神社 奥宮（長野県[[松本市]]安曇上高地） *[[氷鉋斗賣神社]]（長野県[[長野市]][[稲里町 (長野市)|稲里町]]） *[[出水神社]]（[[石川県]][[加賀市]][[橋立町]]） *[[須賀神社 (沼田市)|須賀神社]]（[[群馬県]][[沼田市]]中町） *[[金拆神社]]（[[奈良県]][[桜井市]]大字三輪） *[[海神社 (紀の川市)|海神社]] 境内摂社（[[和歌山県]][[紀の川市]]神領） *[[志賀海神社]] 摂社 今宮神社（[[福岡県]][[福岡市]][[東区 (福岡市)|東区]]） *[[胡簶神社]]（[[長崎県]][[対馬市]][[上対馬町]]）
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}} {{Notelist}}
== 関連項目 == *[[海人族]] *[[海人]]
{{デフォルトソート:うつしひかなさくのみこと}} [[Category:日本の神]] [[Category:国津神]]

== 祀る神社 == *[[穂高神社]] 本宮（[[長野県]][[安曇野市]]穂高） *穂高神社 奥宮（長野県[[松本市]]安曇上高地） *[[氷鉋斗賣神社]]（長野県[[長野市]][[稲里町 (長野市)|稲里町]]） *[[出水神社]]（[[石川県]][[加賀市]][[橋立町]]） *[[須賀神社 (沼田市)|須賀神社]]（[[群馬県]][[沼田市]]中町） *[[金拆神社]]（[[奈良県]][[桜井市]]大字三輪） *[[海神社 (紀の川市)|海神社]] 境内摂社（[[和歌山県]][[紀の川市]]神領） *[[志賀海神社]] 摂社 今宮神社（[[福岡県]][[福岡市]][[東区 (福岡市)|東区]]） *[[胡簶神社]]（[[長崎県]][[対馬市]][[上対馬町]]）

{{ill|Utsushihikanasaku|ja|宇都志日金拆命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushihikanasaku|qq=}} (宇都志日金拆命) is a Japanese deity, son of [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]] and the {{ill|Oyagami|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyagami|qq=}} or tutelary deity of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/utsushihikanasaku/ | title=Utsushihikanasaku • . A History . . Of Japan . 日本歴史|qq=draft|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="explore-azumino-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Explore Azumino! - Hotaka Shrine |url=https://azumino-e-tabi.net/en/sightseeing/hotaka-shrine |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=Explore Azumino! |publisher=[[Japan Tourism Agency]] |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="https-2">https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001562761.pdf</ref><ref name="hikes in japan">{{Cite web |date=2020-10-01 |title=Mt. Hotaka also have deities enshrined, and these deities are as their tutelaries : JINJA-GAKU 3 {{!}} HIKES IN JAPAN |url=https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201001062231/https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-date=2020-10-01 |access-date=2023-12-06}}</ref>
[[original pages]]

He is also known by the name Hotakami no Mikoto.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" /> He is said to have descended to earth on the nearby [[:en:Mount Hotakadake|Mount Hotakadake]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kamikochi - A Place of Special Importance |url=https://www.snowmonkeyresorts.com/activities/kamikochi/a-place-of-special-importance |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=SNOW MONKEY RESORTS}}</ref>

He is considered the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ofune Matsuri – A Unique Festival in Nagano, Japan! - Festivals & Events｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/au3wf7b2 |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}</ref> [[Azumi-no-isora]] is also considered an ancestral deity of them, but has a different name and myth.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora |title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions |year=1993}}</ref>

He is worshipped at {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> In the central Honden.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> It is a prominent [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]<ref name="nussbaum178">[[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA178&dq= "''Engi-shiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 178.</ref>.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> An agricultural shrine in [[:en:Azumino|Azumino]] used by Azumi people who moved inland to become farmers..<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> which also worships his father [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]].<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" />

==Content from [[:en:Azumi-no-isora]]==
{{short description|Shinto kami of the seashore}}

{{More references|date=December 2009}}

{{Expand Japanese|阿曇磯良|date=January 2021}}

{{Nihongo|'''Azumi-no-isora'''|[[:ja:阿曇磯良|阿曇磯良]]|}} is a [[shinto]] [[kami]] of the seashore. He is considered to be the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]]. He is worshiped at a number of shrines, including {{Nihongo|Mekari Shrine|和布刈神社|}} of [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[Shikaumi Shrine]] on [[:en:Shika Island|Shika Island]],<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora|title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions|year=1993}}</ref>
and {{Nihongo|Shiga Shrine|志賀神社|}} of [[:en:Tsushima, Nagasaki|Tsushima]].

Azumi-no-isora is considered a (lesser) sea deity hired as navigator to bring the emissary [[Takenouchi no Sukune]] to the Dragon King (i.e., Dragon God, [[Ryūjin]]) in the late legend regarding the loan of the [[:en:Tide jewels|Tide jewels]] to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]],{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|pp=72–73}} attested in various foundation myth documents of the [[Hachiman]] cult.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=59}}
{{Refn|group="lower-alpha"|According to a certain source, Isora offered his navigational services in "exchange for a sexual relationship" with the empress.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=77}} Though "the motif of divine union between Jingu and a sea god is relatively uncommon", the notion that Azumi-no-isora obtained sexual favors from the empress is attested in shrine-foundation myth ({{illm|jisha engi|ja|寺社縁起|lt=''jisha engi''}}) document called the {{nihongo|''Rokugō kaizan Ninmmon daibosatsu hongi''|六郷開山仁聞大菩薩本紀}}.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=69}} Other shrines of the cult do not promote this idea. Isora carries out the task "without any sexual compensation" in the ''{{illm|Hachiman gudōkun|ja|八幡愚童訓|lt=''Hachiman gudōkun''}} attributed to a priest at the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]].{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=74}}}}

{{Infobox deity
| type = Shinto
| caption = {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}} a major cult center 
| image = File:Hotaka-jinja kaguraden and haiden.jpg
| father = [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]]
| mother = 
| cult_centre = {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}
| cult_center = {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}

| parents = {{ill|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187}}

{{ill|Utsushihikanasaku|ja|宇都志日金拆命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushihikanasaku|qq=}} (宇都志日金拆命) is a Japanese deity, son of [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]] and the {{ill|Oyagami|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyagami|qq=}} or tutelary deity of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/utsushihikanasaku/ | title=Utsushihikanasaku • . A History . . Of Japan . 日本歴史|qq=draft|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="explore-azumino-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Explore Azumino! - Hotaka Shrine |url=https://azumino-e-tabi.net/en/sightseeing/hotaka-shrine |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=Explore Azumino! |publisher=[[Japan Tourism Agency]] |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="https-2">https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001562761.pdf</ref><ref name="hikes in japan">{{Cite web |date=2020-10-01 |title=Mt. Hotaka also have deities enshrined, and these deities are as their tutelaries : JINJA-GAKU 3 {{!}} HIKES IN JAPAN |url=https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201001062231/https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-date=2020-10-01 |access-date=2023-12-06}}</ref>
[[original pages]]

He is also known by the name Hotakami no Mikoto.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" /> He is said to have descended to earth on the nearby [[:en:Mount Hotakadake|Mount Hotakadake]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kamikochi - A Place of Special Importance |url=https://www.snowmonkeyresorts.com/activities/kamikochi/a-place-of-special-importance |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=SNOW MONKEY RESORTS}}</ref>

He is considered the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ofune Matsuri – A Unique Festival in Nagano, Japan! - Festivals & Events｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/au3wf7b2 |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}</ref> [[Azumi-no-isora]] is also considered an ancestral deity of them, but has a different name and myth.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora |title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions |year=1993}}</ref>

He is worshipped at {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> In the central Honden.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> It is a prominent [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]<ref name="nussbaum178">[[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA178&dq= "''Engi-shiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 178.</ref>.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> An agricultural shrine in [[:en:Azumino|Azumino]] used by Azumi people who moved inland to become farmers..<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> which also worships his father [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]].<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" />

==Content from [[:en:Azumi-no-isora]]==
{{short description|Shinto kami of the seashore}}

{{More references|date=December 2009}}

{{Expand Japanese|阿曇磯良|date=January 2021}}

{{Nihongo|'''Azumi-no-isora'''|[[:ja:阿曇磯良|阿曇磯良]]|}} is a [[shinto]] [[kami]] of the seashore. He is considered to be the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]]. He is worshiped at a number of shrines, including {{Nihongo|Mekari Shrine|和布刈神社|}} of [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[Shikaumi Shrine]] on [[:en:Shika Island|Shika Island]],<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora|title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions|year=1993}}</ref>
and {{Nihongo|Shiga Shrine|志賀神社|}} of [[:en:Tsushima, Nagasaki|Tsushima]].

Azumi-no-isora is considered a (lesser) sea deity hired as navigator to bring the emissary [[Takenouchi no Sukune]] to the Dragon King (i.e., Dragon God, [[Ryūjin]]) in the late legend regarding the loan of the [[:en:Tide jewels|Tide jewels]] to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]],{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|pp=72–73}} attested in various foundation myth documents of the [[Hachiman]] cult.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=59}}
{{Refn|group="lower-alpha"|According to a certain source, Isora offered his navigational services in "exchange for a sexual relationship" with the empress.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=77}} Though "the motif of divine union between Jingu and a sea god is relatively uncommon", the notion that Azumi-no-isora obtained sexual favors from the empress is attested in shrine-foundation myth ({{illm|jisha engi|ja|寺社縁起|lt=''jisha engi''}}) document called the {{nihongo|''Rokugō kaizan Ninmmon daibosatsu hongi''|六郷開山仁聞大菩薩本紀}}.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=69}} Other shrines of the cult do not promote this idea. Isora carries out the task "without any sexual compensation" in the ''{{illm|Hachiman gudōkun|ja|八幡愚童訓|lt=''Hachiman gudōkun''}} attributed to a priest at the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]].{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=74}}}}

{{ill|Utsushihikanasaku|ja|宇都志日金拆命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushihikanasaku|qq=}} (宇都志日金拆命) is a Japanese deity, son of [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]] and the {{ill|Oyagami|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyagami|qq=}} or tutelary deity of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/utsushihikanasaku/ | title=Utsushihikanasaku • . A History . . Of Japan . 日本歴史|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="explore-azumino-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Explore Azumino! - Hotaka Shrine |url=https://azumino-e-tabi.net/en/sightseeing/hotaka-shrine |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=Explore Azumino! |publisher=[[Japan Tourism Agency]] |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="https-2">https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001562761.pdf</ref><ref name="hikes in japan">{{Cite web |date=2020-10-01 |title=Mt. Hotaka also have deities enshrined, and these deities are as their tutelaries : JINJA-GAKU 3 {{!}} HIKES IN JAPAN |url=https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201001062231/https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-date=2020-10-01 |access-date=2023-12-06}}</ref>
[[original pages]]

He is also known by the name Hotakami no Mikoto.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" /> He is said to have descended to earth on the nearby [[:en:Mount Hotakadake|Mount Hotakadake]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kamikochi - A Place of Special Importance |url=https://www.snowmonkeyresorts.com/activities/kamikochi/a-place-of-special-importance |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=SNOW MONKEY RESORTS}}</ref>

He is considered the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ofune Matsuri – A Unique Festival in Nagano, Japan! - Festivals & Events｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/au3wf7b2 |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}</ref> [[Azumi-no-isora]] is also considered an ancestral deity of them, but has a different name and myth.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora |title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions |year=1993}}</ref>

He is worshipped at {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> In the central Honden.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> It is a prominent [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]<ref name="nussbaum178">[[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA178&dq= "''Engi-shiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 178.</ref>.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> An agricultural shrine in [[:en:Azumino|Azumino]] used by Azumi people who moved inland to become farmers..<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> which also worships his father [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]].<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" />

==Content from [[:en:Azumi-no-isora]]==
{{short description|Shinto kami of the seashore}}

{{More references|date=December 2009}}

{{Expand Japanese|阿曇磯良|date=January 2021}}

{{Nihongo|'''Azumi-no-isora'''|[[:ja:阿曇磯良|阿曇磯良]]|}} is a [[shinto]] [[kami]] of the seashore. He is considered to be the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]]. He is worshiped at a number of shrines, including {{Nihongo|Mekari Shrine|和布刈神社|}} of [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[Shikaumi Shrine]] on [[:en:Shika Island|Shika Island]],<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora|title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions|year=1993}}</ref>
and {{Nihongo|Shiga Shrine|志賀神社|}} of [[:en:Tsushima, Nagasaki|Tsushima]].

Azumi-no-isora is considered a (lesser) sea deity hired as navigator to bring the emissary [[Takenouchi no Sukune]] to the Dragon King (i.e., Dragon God, [[Ryūjin]]) in the late legend regarding the loan of the [[:en:Tide jewels|Tide jewels]] to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]],{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|pp=72–73}} attested in various foundation myth documents of the [[Hachiman]] cult.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=59}}
{{Refn|group="lower-alpha"|According to a certain source, Isora offered his navigational services in "exchange for a sexual relationship" with the empress.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=77}} Though "the motif of divine union between Jingu and a sea god is relatively uncommon", the notion that Azumi-no-isora obtained sexual favors from the empress is attested in shrine-foundation myth ({{illm|jisha engi|ja|寺社縁起|lt=''jisha engi''}}) document called the {{nihongo|''Rokugō kaizan Ninmmon daibosatsu hongi''|六郷開山仁聞大菩薩本紀}}.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=69}} Other shrines of the cult do not promote this idea. Isora carries out the task "without any sexual compensation" in the ''{{illm|Hachiman gudōkun|ja|八幡愚童訓|lt=''Hachiman gudōkun''}} attributed to a priest at the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]].{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=74}}}}

Of Japan . 日本歴史|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="explore-azumino-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Explore Azumino! - Hotaka Shrine |url=https://azumino-e-tabi.net/en/sightseeing/hotaka-shrine |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=Explore Azumino! |publisher=[[Japan Tourism Agency]] |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="https-2">https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001562761.pdf</ref><ref name="hikes in japan">{{Cite web |date=2020-10-01 |title=Mt. ==Content from [[:en:Azumi-no-isora]]==
{{short description|Shinto kami of the seashore}}

{{More references|date=December 2009}}

{{Expand Japanese|阿曇磯良|date=January 2021}}

{{Nihongo|'''Azumi-no-isora'''|[[:ja:阿曇磯良|阿曇磯良]]|}} is a [[shinto]] [[kami]] of the seashore. He is considered to be the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]]. He is worshiped at a number of shrines, including {{Nihongo|Mekari Shrine|和布刈神社|}} of [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[Shikaumi Shrine]] on [[:en:Shika Island|Shika Island]],<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora|title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions|year=1993}}</ref>
and {{Nihongo|Shiga Shrine|志賀神社|}} of [[:en:Tsushima, Nagasaki|Tsushima]].

Azumi-no-isora is considered a (lesser) sea deity hired as navigator to bring the emissary [[Takenouchi no Sukune]] to the Dragon King (i.e., Dragon God, [[Ryūjin]]) in the late legend regarding the loan of the [[:en:Tide jewels|Tide jewels]] to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]],{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|pp=72–73}} attested in various foundation myth documents of the [[Hachiman]] cult.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=59}}
{{Refn|group="lower-alpha"|According to a certain source, Isora offered his navigational services in "exchange for a sexual relationship" with the empress.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=77}} Though "the motif of divine union between Jingu and a sea god is relatively uncommon", the notion that Azumi-no-isora obtained sexual favors from the empress is attested in shrine-foundation myth ({{illm|jisha engi|ja|寺社縁起|lt=''jisha engi''}}) document called the {{nihongo|''Rokugō kaizan Ninmmon daibosatsu hongi''|六郷開山仁聞大菩薩本紀}}.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=69}} Other shrines of the cult do not promote this idea. Isora carries out the task "without any sexual compensation" in the ''{{illm|Hachiman gudōkun|ja|八幡愚童訓|lt=''Hachiman gudōkun''}} attributed to a priest at the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]].{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=74}}}}

{{Infobox deity
| type = Shinto
| caption = {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}} a major cult center 
| image = File:Hotaka-jinja kaguraden and haiden.jpg
| father = [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]]
| mother = 
| cult_centre = {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}
| cult_center = {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}

| parents = {{ill|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187}}

{{ill|Utsushihikanasaku|ja|宇都志日金拆命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushihikanasaku|qq=}} (宇都志日金拆命) is a Japanese deity, son of [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]] and the {{ill|Oyagami|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyagami|qq=}} or tutelary deity of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/utsushihikanasaku/ | title=Utsushihikanasaku • . A History . . Of Japan . 日本歴史|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="explore-azumino-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Explore Azumino! - Hotaka Shrine |url=https://azumino-e-tabi.net/en/sightseeing/hotaka-shrine |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=Explore Azumino! |publisher=[[Japan Tourism Agency]] |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="https-2">https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001562761.pdf</ref><ref name="hikes in japan">{{Cite web |date=2020-10-01 |title=Mt. Hotaka also have deities enshrined, and these deities are as their tutelaries : JINJA-GAKU 3 {{!}} HIKES IN JAPAN |url=https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201001062231/https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-date=2020-10-01 |access-date=2023-12-06}}</ref>
[[original pages]]

He is also known by the name Hotakami no Mikoto.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" /> He is said to have descended to earth on the nearby [[:en:Mount Hotakadake|Mount Hotakadake]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kamikochi - A Place of Special Importance |url=https://www.snowmonkeyresorts.com/activities/kamikochi/a-place-of-special-importance |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=SNOW MONKEY RESORTS}}</ref>

He is considered the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ofune Matsuri – A Unique Festival in Nagano, Japan! - Festivals & Events｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/au3wf7b2 |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}</ref> [[Azumi-no-isora]] is also considered an ancestral deity of them, but has a different name and myth.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora |title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions |year=1993}}</ref>

He is worshipped at {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> In the central Honden.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> It is a prominent [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]<ref name="nussbaum178">[[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA178&dq= "''Engi-shiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 178.</ref>.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> An agricultural shrine in [[:en:Azumino|Azumino]] used by Azumi people who moved inland to become farmers..<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> which also worships his father [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]].<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" />

==Content from [[:en:Azumi-no-isora]]==
{{short description|Shinto kami of the seashore}}

{{More references|date=December 2009}}

{{Expand Japanese|阿曇磯良|date=January 2021}}

{{Nihongo|'''Azumi-no-isora'''|[[:ja:阿曇磯良|阿曇磯良]]|}} is a [[shinto]] [[kami]] of the seashore. He is considered to be the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]]. He is worshiped at a number of shrines, including {{Nihongo|Mekari Shrine|和布刈神社|}} of [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[Shikaumi Shrine]] on [[:en:Shika Island|Shika Island]],<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora|title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions|year=1993}}</ref>
and {{Nihongo|Shiga Shrine|志賀神社|}} of [[:en:Tsushima, Nagasaki|Tsushima]].

Azumi-no-isora is considered a (lesser) sea deity hired as navigator to bring the emissary [[Takenouchi no Sukune]] to the Dragon King (i.e., Dragon God, [[Ryūjin]]) in the late legend regarding the loan of the [[:en:Tide jewels|Tide jewels]] to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]],{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|pp=72–73}} attested in various foundation myth documents of the [[Hachiman]] cult.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=59}}
{{Refn|group="lower-alpha"|According to a certain source, Isora offered his navigational services in "exchange for a sexual relationship" with the empress.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=77}} Though "the motif of divine union between Jingu and a sea god is relatively uncommon", the notion that Azumi-no-isora obtained sexual favors from the empress is attested in shrine-foundation myth ({{illm|jisha engi|ja|寺社縁起|lt=''jisha engi''}}) document called the {{nihongo|''Rokugō kaizan Ninmmon daibosatsu hongi''|六郷開山仁聞大菩薩本紀}}.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=69}} Other shrines of the cult do not promote this idea. Isora carries out the task "without any sexual compensation" in the ''{{illm|Hachiman gudōkun|ja|八幡愚童訓|lt=''Hachiman gudōkun''}} attributed to a priest at the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]].{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=74}}}}

{{ill|Utsushihikanasaku|ja|宇都志日金拆命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushihikanasaku|qq=}} (宇都志日金拆命) is a Japanese deity, son of [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]] and the {{ill|Oyagami|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyagami|qq=}} or tutelary deity of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/utsushihikanasaku/ | title=Utsushihikanasaku • . A History . . Of Japan . 日本歴史|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="explore-azumino-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Explore Azumino! - Hotaka Shrine |url=https://azumino-e-tabi.net/en/sightseeing/hotaka-shrine |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=Explore Azumino! |publisher=[[Japan Tourism Agency]] |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="https-2">https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001562761.pdf</ref><ref name="hikes in japan">{{Cite web |date=2020-10-01 |title=Mt. Hotaka also have deities enshrined, and these deities are as their tutelaries : JINJA-GAKU 3 {{!}} HIKES IN JAPAN |url=https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201001062231/https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-date=2020-10-01 |access-date=2023-12-06}}</ref>
[[original pages]]

He is also known by the name Hotakami no Mikoto.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" /> He is said to have descended to earth on the nearby [[:en:Mount Hotakadake|Mount Hotakadake]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kamikochi - A Place of Special Importance |url=https://www.snowmonkeyresorts.com/activities/kamikochi/a-place-of-special-importance |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=SNOW MONKEY RESORTS}}</ref>

He is considered the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ofune Matsuri – A Unique Festival in Nagano, Japan! - Festivals & Events｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/au3wf7b2 |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}</ref> [[Azumi-no-isora]] is also considered an ancestral deity of them, but has a different name and myth.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora |title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions |year=1993}}</ref>

He is worshipped at {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> In the central Honden.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> It is a prominent [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]<ref name="nussbaum178">[[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA178&dq= "''Engi-shiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 178.</ref>.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> An agricultural shrine in [[:en:Azumino|Azumino]] used by Azumi people who moved inland to become farmers..<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> which also worships his father [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]].<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" />

==Content from [[:en:Azumi-no-isora]]==
{{short description|Shinto kami of the seashore}}

{{More references|date=December 2009}}

{{Expand Japanese|阿曇磯良|date=January 2021}}

{{Nihongo|'''Azumi-no-isora'''|[[:ja:阿曇磯良|阿曇磯良]]|}} is a [[shinto]] [[kami]] of the seashore. He is considered to be the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]]. He is worshiped at a number of shrines, including {{Nihongo|Mekari Shrine|和布刈神社|}} of [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[Shikaumi Shrine]] on [[:en:Shika Island|Shika Island]],<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora|title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions|year=1993}}</ref>
and {{Nihongo|Shiga Shrine|志賀神社|}} of [[:en:Tsushima, Nagasaki|Tsushima]].

Azumi-no-isora is considered a (lesser) sea deity hired as navigator to bring the emissary [[Takenouchi no Sukune]] to the Dragon King (i.e., Dragon God, [[Ryūjin]]) in the late legend regarding the loan of the [[:en:Tide jewels|Tide jewels]] to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]],{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|pp=72–73}} attested in various foundation myth documents of the [[Hachiman]] cult.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=59}}
{{Refn|group="lower-alpha"|According to a certain source, Isora offered his navigational services in "exchange for a sexual relationship" with the empress.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=77}} Though "the motif of divine union between Jingu and a sea god is relatively uncommon", the notion that Azumi-no-isora obtained sexual favors from the empress is attested in shrine-foundation myth ({{illm|jisha engi|ja|寺社縁起|lt=''jisha engi''}}) document called the {{nihongo|''Rokugō kaizan Ninmmon daibosatsu hongi''|六郷開山仁聞大菩薩本紀}}.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=69}} Other shrines of the cult do not promote this idea. Isora carries out the task "without any sexual compensation" in the ''{{illm|Hachiman gudōkun|ja|八幡愚童訓|lt=''Hachiman gudōkun''}} attributed to a priest at the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]].{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=74}}}}

==Content from [[:en:Azumi-no-isora]]==
{{short description|Shinto kami of the seashore}}

{{More references|date=December 2009}}

{{Expand Japanese|阿曇磯良|date=January 2021}}

{{Nihongo|'''Azumi-no-isora'''|[[:ja:阿曇磯良|阿曇磯良]]|}} is a [[shinto]] [[kami]] of the seashore. He is considered to be the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]]. He is worshiped at a number of shrines, including {{Nihongo|Mekari Shrine|和布刈神社|}} of [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[Shikaumi Shrine]] on [[:en:Shika Island|Shika Island]],<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora|title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions|year=1993}}</ref>
and {{Nihongo|Shiga Shrine|志賀神社|}} of [[:en:Tsushima, Nagasaki|Tsushima]].

Azumi-no-isora is considered a (lesser) sea deity hired as navigator to bring the emissary [[Takenouchi no Sukune]] to the Dragon King (i.e., Dragon God, [[Ryūjin]]) in the late legend regarding the loan of the [[:en:Tide jewels|Tide jewels]] to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]],{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|pp=72–73}} attested in various foundation myth documents of the [[Hachiman]] cult.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=59}}
{{Refn|group="lower-alpha"|According to a certain source, Isora offered his navigational services in "exchange for a sexual relationship" with the empress.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=77}} Though "the motif of divine union between Jingu and a sea god is relatively uncommon", the notion that Azumi-no-isora obtained sexual favors from the empress is attested in shrine-foundation myth ({{illm|jisha engi|ja|寺社縁起|lt=''jisha engi''}}) document called the {{nihongo|''Rokugō kaizan Ninmmon daibosatsu hongi''|六郷開山仁聞大菩薩本紀}}.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=69}} Other shrines of the cult do not promote this idea. Isora carries out the task "without any sexual compensation" in the ''{{illm|Hachiman gudōkun|ja|八幡愚童訓|lt=''Hachiman gudōkun''}} attributed to a priest at the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]].{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=74}}}}

{{ill|Utsushihikanasaku|ja|宇都志日金拆命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushihikanasaku|qq=}} (宇都志日金拆命) is a Japanese deity, son of [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]] and the {{ill|Oyagami|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyagami|qq=}} or tutelary deity of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/utsushihikanasaku/ | title=Utsushihikanasaku • . A History . . Of Japan . 日本歴史|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="explore-azumino-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Explore Azumino! - Hotaka Shrine |url=https://azumino-e-tabi.net/en/sightseeing/hotaka-shrine |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=Explore Azumino! |publisher=[[Japan Tourism Agency]] |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="https-2">https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001562761.pdf</ref><ref name="hikes in japan">{{Cite web |date=2020-10-01 |title=Mt. Hotaka also have deities enshrined, and these deities are as their tutelaries : JINJA-GAKU 3 {{!}} HIKES IN JAPAN |url=https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201001062231/https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-date=2020-10-01 |access-date=2023-12-06}}</ref>
[[original pages]]

He is also known by the name Hotakami no Mikoto.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" /> He is said to have descended to earth on the nearby [[:en:Mount Hotakadake|Mount Hotakadake]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kamikochi - A Place of Special Importance |url=https://www.snowmonkeyresorts.com/activities/kamikochi/a-place-of-special-importance |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=SNOW MONKEY RESORTS}}</ref>

He is considered the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ofune Matsuri – A Unique Festival in Nagano, Japan! - Festivals & Events｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/au3wf7b2 |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}</ref> [[Azumi-no-isora]] is also considered an ancestral deity of them, but has a different name and myth.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora |title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions |year=1993}}</ref>

He is worshipped at {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> In the central Honden.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> It is a prominent [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]<ref name="nussbaum178">[[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA178&dq= "''Engi-shiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 178.</ref>.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> An agricultural shrine in [[:en:Azumino|Azumino]] used by Azumi people who moved inland to become farmers..<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> which also worships his father [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]].<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" />

==Content from [[:en:Azumi-no-isora]]==
{{short description|Shinto kami of the seashore}}

{{More references|date=December 2009}}

{{Expand Japanese|阿曇磯良|date=January 2021}}

{{Nihongo|'''Azumi-no-isora'''|[[:ja:阿曇磯良|阿曇磯良]]|}} is a [[shinto]] [[kami]] of the seashore. He is considered to be the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]]. He is worshiped at a number of shrines, including {{Nihongo|Mekari Shrine|和布刈神社|}} of [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[Shikaumi Shrine]] on [[:en:Shika Island|Shika Island]],<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora|title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions|year=1993}}</ref>
and {{Nihongo|Shiga Shrine|志賀神社|}} of [[:en:Tsushima, Nagasaki|Tsushima]].

Azumi-no-isora is considered a (lesser) sea deity hired as navigator to bring the emissary [[Takenouchi no Sukune]] to the Dragon King (i.e., Dragon God, [[Ryūjin]]) in the late legend regarding the loan of the [[:en:Tide jewels|Tide jewels]] to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]],{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|pp=72–73}} attested in various foundation myth documents of the [[Hachiman]] cult.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=59}}
{{Refn|group="lower-alpha"|According to a certain source, Isora offered his navigational services in "exchange for a sexual relationship" with the empress.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=77}} Though "the motif of divine union between Jingu and a sea god is relatively uncommon", the notion that Azumi-no-isora obtained sexual favors from the empress is attested in shrine-foundation myth ({{illm|jisha engi|ja|寺社縁起|lt=''jisha engi''}}) document called the {{nihongo|''Rokugō kaizan Ninmmon daibosatsu hongi''|六郷開山仁聞大菩薩本紀}}.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=69}} Other shrines of the cult do not promote this idea. Isora carries out the task "without any sexual compensation" in the ''{{illm|Hachiman gudōkun|ja|八幡愚童訓|lt=''Hachiman gudōkun''}} attributed to a priest at the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]].{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=74}}}}

He is also known by the name Hotakami no Mikoto.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" /> He is said to have descended to earth on the nearby [[:en:Mount Hotakadake|Mount Hotakadake]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kamikochi - A Place of Special Importance |url=https://www.snowmonkeyresorts.com/activities/kamikochi/a-place-of-special-importance |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=SNOW MONKEY RESORTS}}</ref>

He is considered the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ofune Matsuri – A Unique Festival in Nagano, Japan! - Festivals & Events｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/au3wf7b2 |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}</ref> [[Azumi-no-isora]] is also considered an ancestral deity of them, but has a different name and myth.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora |title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions |year=1993}}</ref>

He is worshipped at {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> In the central Honden.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> It is a prominent [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]<ref name="nussbaum178">[[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA178&dq= "''Engi-shiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 178.</ref>.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> An agricultural shrine in [[:en:Azumino|Azumino]] used by Azumi people who moved inland to become farmers..<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> which also worships his father [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]].<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" />

==Content from [[:en:Azumi-no-isora]]==
{{short description|Shinto kami of the seashore}}

{{More references|date=December 2009}}

{{Expand Japanese|阿曇磯良|date=January 2021}}

{{Nihongo|'''Azumi-no-isora'''|[[:ja:阿曇磯良|阿曇磯良]]|}} is a [[shinto]] [[kami]] of the seashore. He is considered to be the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]]. He is worshiped at a number of shrines, including {{Nihongo|Mekari Shrine|和布刈神社|}} of [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[Shikaumi Shrine]] on [[:en:Shika Island|Shika Island]],<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora|title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions|year=1993}}</ref>
and {{Nihongo|Shiga Shrine|志賀神社|}} of [[:en:Tsushima, Nagasaki|Tsushima]].

Azumi-no-isora is considered a (lesser) sea deity hired as navigator to bring the emissary [[Takenouchi no Sukune]] to the Dragon King (i.e., Dragon God, [[Ryūjin]]) in the late legend regarding the loan of the [[:en:Tide jewels|Tide jewels]] to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]],{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|pp=72–73}} attested in various foundation myth documents of the [[Hachiman]] cult.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=59}}
{{Refn|group="lower-alpha"|According to a certain source, Isora offered his navigational services in "exchange for a sexual relationship" with the empress.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=77}} Though "the motif of divine union between Jingu and a sea god is relatively uncommon", the notion that Azumi-no-isora obtained sexual favors from the empress is attested in shrine-foundation myth ({{illm|jisha engi|ja|寺社縁起|lt=''jisha engi''}}) document called the {{nihongo|''Rokugō kaizan Ninmmon daibosatsu hongi''|六郷開山仁聞大菩薩本紀}}.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=69}} Other shrines of the cult do not promote this idea. Isora carries out the task "without any sexual compensation" in the ''{{illm|Hachiman gudōkun|ja|八幡愚童訓|lt=''Hachiman gudōkun''}} attributed to a priest at the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]].{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=74}}}}

{{ill|Utsushihikanasaku|ja|宇都志日金拆命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushihikanasaku|qq=}} (宇都志日金拆命) is a Japanese deity, son of [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]] and the {{ill|Oyagami|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyagami|qq=}} or tutelary deity of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/utsushihikanasaku/ | title=Utsushihikanasaku • . A History . . Of Japan . 日本歴史|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="explore-azumino-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Explore Azumino! - Hotaka Shrine |url=https://azumino-e-tabi.net/en/sightseeing/hotaka-shrine |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=Explore Azumino! |publisher=[[Japan Tourism Agency]] |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="https-2">https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001562761.pdf</ref><ref name="hikes in japan">{{Cite web |date=2020-10-01 |title=Mt. Hotaka also have deities enshrined, and these deities are as their tutelaries : JINJA-GAKU 3 {{!}} HIKES IN JAPAN |url=https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201001062231/https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-date=2020-10-01 |access-date=2023-12-06}}</ref>
[[original pages]]

He is also known by the name Hotakami no Mikoto.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" /> He is said to have descended to earth on the nearby [[:en:Mount Hotakadake|Mount Hotakadake]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kamikochi - A Place of Special Importance |url=https://www.snowmonkeyresorts.com/activities/kamikochi/a-place-of-special-importance |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=SNOW MONKEY RESORTS}}</ref>

He is considered the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ofune Matsuri – A Unique Festival in Nagano, Japan! - Festivals & Events｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/au3wf7b2 |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}</ref> [[Azumi-no-isora]] is also considered an ancestral deity of them, but has a different name and myth.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora |title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions |year=1993}}</ref>

He is worshipped at {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> In the central Honden.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> It is a prominent [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]<ref name="nussbaum178">[[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA178&dq= "''Engi-shiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 178.</ref>.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> An agricultural shrine in [[:en:Azumino|Azumino]] used by Azumi people who moved inland to become farmers..<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> which also worships his father [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]].<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" />

==Content from [[:en:Azumi-no-isora]]==
{{short description|Shinto kami of the seashore}}

{{More references|date=December 2009}}

{{Expand Japanese|阿曇磯良|date=January 2021}}

{{Nihongo|'''Azumi-no-isora'''|[[:ja:阿曇磯良|阿曇磯良]]|}} is a [[shinto]] [[kami]] of the seashore. He is considered to be the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]]. He is worshiped at a number of shrines, including {{Nihongo|Mekari Shrine|和布刈神社|}} of [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[Shikaumi Shrine]] on [[:en:Shika Island|Shika Island]],<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora|title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions|year=1993}}</ref>
and {{Nihongo|Shiga Shrine|志賀神社|}} of [[:en:Tsushima, Nagasaki|Tsushima]].

Azumi-no-isora is considered a (lesser) sea deity hired as navigator to bring the emissary [[Takenouchi no Sukune]] to the Dragon King (i.e., Dragon God, [[Ryūjin]]) in the late legend regarding the loan of the [[:en:Tide jewels|Tide jewels]] to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]],{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|pp=72–73}} attested in various foundation myth documents of the [[Hachiman]] cult.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=59}}
{{Refn|group="lower-alpha"|According to a certain source, Isora offered his navigational services in "exchange for a sexual relationship" with the empress.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=77}} Though "the motif of divine union between Jingu and a sea god is relatively uncommon", the notion that Azumi-no-isora obtained sexual favors from the empress is attested in shrine-foundation myth ({{illm|jisha engi|ja|寺社縁起|lt=''jisha engi''}}) document called the {{nihongo|''Rokugō kaizan Ninmmon daibosatsu hongi''|六郷開山仁聞大菩薩本紀}}.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=69}} Other shrines of the cult do not promote this idea. Isora carries out the task "without any sexual compensation" in the ''{{illm|Hachiman gudōkun|ja|八幡愚童訓|lt=''Hachiman gudōkun''}} attributed to a priest at the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]].{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=74}}}}

| type = Shinto
| caption = {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}} a major cult center 
| image = File:Hotaka-jinja kaguraden and haiden.jpg
| father = [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]]
| mother = 
| cult_centre = {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}
| cult_center = {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}

| parents = {{ill|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187}}

{{ill|Utsushihikanasaku|ja|宇都志日金拆命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushihikanasaku|qq=}} (宇都志日金拆命) is a Japanese deity, son of [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]] and the {{ill|Oyagami|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyagami|qq=}} or tutelary deity of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/utsushihikanasaku/ | title=Utsushihikanasaku • . A History . . Of Japan . 日本歴史|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="explore-azumino-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Explore Azumino! - Hotaka Shrine |url=https://azumino-e-tabi.net/en/sightseeing/hotaka-shrine |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=Explore Azumino! |publisher=[[Japan Tourism Agency]] |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="https-2">https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001562761.pdf</ref><ref name="hikes in japan">{{Cite web |date=2020-10-01 |title=Mt. Hotaka also have deities enshrined, and these deities are as their tutelaries : JINJA-GAKU 3 {{!}} HIKES IN JAPAN |url=https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201001062231/https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-date=2020-10-01 |access-date=2023-12-06}}</ref>
[[original pages]]

He is also known by the name Hotakami no Mikoto.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" /> He is said to have descended to earth on the nearby [[:en:Mount Hotakadake|Mount Hotakadake]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kamikochi - A Place of Special Importance |url=https://www.snowmonkeyresorts.com/activities/kamikochi/a-place-of-special-importance |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=SNOW MONKEY RESORTS}}</ref>

He is considered the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ofune Matsuri – A Unique Festival in Nagano, Japan! - Festivals & Events｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/au3wf7b2 |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}</ref> [[Azumi-no-isora]] is also considered an ancestral deity of them, but has a different name and myth.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora |title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions |year=1993}}</ref>

He is worshipped at {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> In the central Honden.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> It is a prominent [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]<ref name="nussbaum178">[[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA178&dq= "''Engi-shiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 178.</ref>.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> An agricultural shrine in [[:en:Azumino|Azumino]] used by Azumi people who moved inland to become farmers..<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> which also worships his father [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]].<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" />

==Content from [[:en:Azumi-no-isora]]==
{{short description|Shinto kami of the seashore}}

{{More references|date=December 2009}}

{{Expand Japanese|阿曇磯良|date=January 2021}}

{{Nihongo|'''Azumi-no-isora'''|[[:ja:阿曇磯良|阿曇磯良]]|}} is a [[shinto]] [[kami]] of the seashore. He is considered to be the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]]. He is worshiped at a number of shrines, including {{Nihongo|Mekari Shrine|和布刈神社|}} of [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[Shikaumi Shrine]] on [[:en:Shika Island|Shika Island]],<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora|title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions|year=1993}}</ref>
and {{Nihongo|Shiga Shrine|志賀神社|}} of [[:en:Tsushima, Nagasaki|Tsushima]].

Azumi-no-isora is considered a (lesser) sea deity hired as navigator to bring the emissary [[Takenouchi no Sukune]] to the Dragon King (i.e., Dragon God, [[Ryūjin]]) in the late legend regarding the loan of the [[:en:Tide jewels|Tide jewels]] to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]],{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|pp=72–73}} attested in various foundation myth documents of the [[Hachiman]] cult.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=59}}
{{Refn|group="lower-alpha"|According to a certain source, Isora offered his navigational services in "exchange for a sexual relationship" with the empress.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=77}} Though "the motif of divine union between Jingu and a sea god is relatively uncommon", the notion that Azumi-no-isora obtained sexual favors from the empress is attested in shrine-foundation myth ({{illm|jisha engi|ja|寺社縁起|lt=''jisha engi''}}) document called the {{nihongo|''Rokugō kaizan Ninmmon daibosatsu hongi''|六郷開山仁聞大菩薩本紀}}.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=69}} Other shrines of the cult do not promote this idea. Isora carries out the task "without any sexual compensation" in the ''{{illm|Hachiman gudōkun|ja|八幡愚童訓|lt=''Hachiman gudōkun''}} attributed to a priest at the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]].{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=74}}}}

{{ill|Utsushihikanasaku|ja|宇都志日金拆命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushihikanasaku|qq=}} (宇都志日金拆命) is a Japanese deity, son of [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]] and the {{ill|Oyagami|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyagami|qq=}} or tutelary deity of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/utsushihikanasaku/ | title=Utsushihikanasaku • . A History . . Of Japan . 日本歴史|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="explore-azumino-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Explore Azumino! - Hotaka Shrine |url=https://azumino-e-tabi.net/en/sightseeing/hotaka-shrine |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=Explore Azumino! |publisher=[[Japan Tourism Agency]] |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="https-2">https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001562761.pdf</ref><ref name="hikes in japan">{{Cite web |date=2020-10-01 |title=Mt. [[original pages]]

He is also known by the name Hotakami no Mikoto.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" /> He is said to have descended to earth on the nearby [[:en:Mount Hotakadake|Mount Hotakadake]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kamikochi - A Place of Special Importance |url=https://www.snowmonkeyresorts.com/activities/kamikochi/a-place-of-special-importance |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=SNOW MONKEY RESORTS}}</ref>

He is considered the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ofune Matsuri – A Unique Festival in Nagano, Japan! - Festivals & Events｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/au3wf7b2 |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}</ref> [[Azumi-no-isora]] is also considered an ancestral deity of them, but has a different name and myth.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora |title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions |year=1993}}</ref>

He is worshipped at {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> In the central Honden.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> It is a prominent [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]<ref name="nussbaum178">[[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA178&dq= "''Engi-shiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 178.</ref>.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> An agricultural shrine in [[:en:Azumino|Azumino]] used by Azumi people who moved inland to become farmers..<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> which also worships his father [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]].<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" />

==Content from [[:en:Azumi-no-isora]]==
{{short description|Shinto kami of the seashore}}

{{More references|date=December 2009}}

{{Expand Japanese|阿曇磯良|date=January 2021}}

{{Nihongo|'''Azumi-no-isora'''|[[:ja:阿曇磯良|阿曇磯良]]|}} is a [[shinto]] [[kami]] of the seashore. He is considered to be the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]]. He is worshiped at a number of shrines, including {{Nihongo|Mekari Shrine|和布刈神社|}} of [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[Shikaumi Shrine]] on [[:en:Shika Island|Shika Island]],<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora|title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions|year=1993}}</ref>
and {{Nihongo|Shiga Shrine|志賀神社|}} of [[:en:Tsushima, Nagasaki|Tsushima]].

Azumi-no-isora is considered a (lesser) sea deity hired as navigator to bring the emissary [[Takenouchi no Sukune]] to the Dragon King (i.e., Dragon God, [[Ryūjin]]) in the late legend regarding the loan of the [[:en:Tide jewels|Tide jewels]] to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]],{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|pp=72–73}} attested in various foundation myth documents of the [[Hachiman]] cult.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=59}}
{{Refn|group="lower-alpha"|According to a certain source, Isora offered his navigational services in "exchange for a sexual relationship" with the empress.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=77}} Though "the motif of divine union between Jingu and a sea god is relatively uncommon", the notion that Azumi-no-isora obtained sexual favors from the empress is attested in shrine-foundation myth ({{illm|jisha engi|ja|寺社縁起|lt=''jisha engi''}}) document called the {{nihongo|''Rokugō kaizan Ninmmon daibosatsu hongi''|六郷開山仁聞大菩薩本紀}}.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=69}} Other shrines of the cult do not promote this idea. Isora carries out the task "without any sexual compensation" in the ''{{illm|Hachiman gudōkun|ja|八幡愚童訓|lt=''Hachiman gudōkun''}} attributed to a priest at the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]].{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=74}}}}

{{ill|Utsushihikanasaku|ja|宇都志日金拆命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushihikanasaku|qq=}} (宇都志日金拆命) is a Japanese deity, son of [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]] and the {{ill|Oyagami|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyagami|qq=}} or tutelary deity of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/utsushihikanasaku/ | title=Utsushihikanasaku • . A History . . Of Japan . 日本歴史|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="explore-azumino-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Explore Azumino! - Hotaka Shrine |url=https://azumino-e-tabi.net/en/sightseeing/hotaka-shrine |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=Explore Azumino! |publisher=[[Japan Tourism Agency]] |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="https-2">https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001562761.pdf</ref><ref name="hikes in japan">{{Cite web |date=2020-10-01 |title=Mt. Hotaka also have deities enshrined, and these deities are as their tutelaries : JINJA-GAKU 3 {{!}} HIKES IN JAPAN |url=https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201001062231/https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-date=2020-10-01 |access-date=2023-12-06}}</ref>
[[original pages]]

He is also known by the name Hotakami no Mikoto.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" /> He is said to have descended to earth on the nearby [[:en:Mount Hotakadake|Mount Hotakadake]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kamikochi - A Place of Special Importance |url=https://www.snowmonkeyresorts.com/activities/kamikochi/a-place-of-special-importance |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=SNOW MONKEY RESORTS}}</ref>

He is considered the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ofune Matsuri – A Unique Festival in Nagano, Japan! - Festivals & Events｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/au3wf7b2 |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}</ref> [[Azumi-no-isora]] is also considered an ancestral deity of them, but has a different name and myth.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora |title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions |year=1993}}</ref>

He is worshipped at {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> In the central Honden.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> It is a prominent [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]<ref name="nussbaum178">[[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA178&dq= "''Engi-shiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 178.</ref>.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> An agricultural shrine in [[:en:Azumino|Azumino]] used by Azumi people who moved inland to become farmers..<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> which also worships his father [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]].<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" />

==Content from [[:en:Azumi-no-isora]]==
{{short description|Shinto kami of the seashore}}

{{More references|date=December 2009}}

{{Expand Japanese|阿曇磯良|date=January 2021}}

{{Nihongo|'''Azumi-no-isora'''|[[:ja:阿曇磯良|阿曇磯良]]|}} is a [[shinto]] [[kami]] of the seashore. He is considered to be the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]]. He is worshiped at a number of shrines, including {{Nihongo|Mekari Shrine|和布刈神社|}} of [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[Shikaumi Shrine]] on [[:en:Shika Island|Shika Island]],<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora|title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions|year=1993}}</ref>
and {{Nihongo|Shiga Shrine|志賀神社|}} of [[:en:Tsushima, Nagasaki|Tsushima]].

Azumi-no-isora is considered a (lesser) sea deity hired as navigator to bring the emissary [[Takenouchi no Sukune]] to the Dragon King (i.e., Dragon God, [[Ryūjin]]) in the late legend regarding the loan of the [[:en:Tide jewels|Tide jewels]] to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]],{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|pp=72–73}} attested in various foundation myth documents of the [[Hachiman]] cult.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=59}}
{{Refn|group="lower-alpha"|According to a certain source, Isora offered his navigational services in "exchange for a sexual relationship" with the empress.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=77}} Though "the motif of divine union between Jingu and a sea god is relatively uncommon", the notion that Azumi-no-isora obtained sexual favors from the empress is attested in shrine-foundation myth ({{illm|jisha engi|ja|寺社縁起|lt=''jisha engi''}}) document called the {{nihongo|''Rokugō kaizan Ninmmon daibosatsu hongi''|六郷開山仁聞大菩薩本紀}}.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=69}} Other shrines of the cult do not promote this idea. Isora carries out the task "without any sexual compensation" in the ''{{illm|Hachiman gudōkun|ja|八幡愚童訓|lt=''Hachiman gudōkun''}} attributed to a priest at the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]].{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=74}}}}

{{Infobox deity
| type = Shinto
| caption = {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}} a major cult center 
| image = File:Hotaka-jinja kaguraden and haiden.jpg
| father = [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]]
| mother = 
| cult_centre = {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}
| cult_center = {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}
| gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}
| parents = {{ill|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187}}
| equivalent5_type = Japanese | equivalent5 = {{ill|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187}}
}}

{{ill|Utsushihikanasaku|ja|宇都志日金拆命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushihikanasaku|qq=}} (宇都志日金拆命) is a Japanese deity, son of [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]] and the {{ill|Oyagami|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyagami|qq=}} or tutelary deity of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/utsushihikanasaku/ | title=Utsushihikanasaku • . A History . . Of Japan . 日本歴史|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="explore-azumino-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Explore Azumino! - Hotaka Shrine |url=https://azumino-e-tabi.net/en/sightseeing/hotaka-shrine |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=Explore Azumino! |publisher=[[Japan Tourism Agency]] |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="https-2">https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001562761.pdf</ref><ref name="hikes in japan">{{Cite web |date=2020-10-01 |title=Mt. Hotaka also have deities enshrined, and these deities are as their tutelaries : JINJA-GAKU 3 {{!}} HIKES IN JAPAN |url=https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201001062231/https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-date=2020-10-01 |access-date=2023-12-06}}</ref>
[[original pages]]

He is also known by the name Hotakami no Mikoto.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" /> He is said to have descended to earth on the nearby [[:en:Mount Hotakadake|Mount Hotakadake]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kamikochi - A Place of Special Importance |url=https://www.snowmonkeyresorts.com/activities/kamikochi/a-place-of-special-importance |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=SNOW MONKEY RESORTS}}</ref>

He is considered the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ofune Matsuri – A Unique Festival in Nagano, Japan! - Festivals & Events｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/au3wf7b2 |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}</ref> [[Azumi-no-isora]] is also considered an ancestral deity of them, but has a different name and myth.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora |title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions |year=1993}}</ref>

He is worshipped at {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> In the central Honden.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> It is a prominent [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]<ref name="nussbaum178">[[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA178&dq= "''Engi-shiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 178.</ref>.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> An agricultural shrine in [[:en:Azumino|Azumino]] used by Azumi people who moved inland to become farmers..<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> which also worships his father [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]].<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" />

==Content from [[:en:Azumi-no-isora]]==
{{short description|Shinto kami of the seashore}}

{{More references|date=December 2009}}

{{Expand Japanese|阿曇磯良|date=January 2021}}

{{Nihongo|'''Azumi-no-isora'''|[[:ja:阿曇磯良|阿曇磯良]]|}} is a [[shinto]] [[kami]] of the seashore. He is considered to be the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]]. He is worshiped at a number of shrines, including {{Nihongo|Mekari Shrine|和布刈神社|}} of [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[Shikaumi Shrine]] on [[:en:Shika Island|Shika Island]],<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora|title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions|year=1993}}</ref>
and {{Nihongo|Shiga Shrine|志賀神社|}} of [[:en:Tsushima, Nagasaki|Tsushima]].

Azumi-no-isora is considered a (lesser) sea deity hired as navigator to bring the emissary [[Takenouchi no Sukune]] to the Dragon King (i.e., Dragon God, [[Ryūjin]]) in the late legend regarding the loan of the [[:en:Tide jewels|Tide jewels]] to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]],{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|pp=72–73}} attested in various foundation myth documents of the [[Hachiman]] cult.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=59}}
{{Refn|group="lower-alpha"|According to a certain source, Isora offered his navigational services in "exchange for a sexual relationship" with the empress.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=77}} Though "the motif of divine union between Jingu and a sea god is relatively uncommon", the notion that Azumi-no-isora obtained sexual favors from the empress is attested in shrine-foundation myth ({{illm|jisha engi|ja|寺社縁起|lt=''jisha engi''}}) document called the {{nihongo|''Rokugō kaizan Ninmmon daibosatsu hongi''|六郷開山仁聞大菩薩本紀}}.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=69}} Other shrines of the cult do not promote this idea. Isora carries out the task "without any sexual compensation" in the ''{{illm|Hachiman gudōkun|ja|八幡愚童訓|lt=''Hachiman gudōkun''}} attributed to a priest at the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]].{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=74}}}}
