Hotaka also have deities enshrined, and these deities are as their tutelaries : JINJA-GAKU 3 {{!}} HIKES IN JAPAN |url=https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201001062231/https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-date=2020-10-01 |access-date=2023-12-06}}</ref>
[[original pages]]

He is also known by the name Hotakami no Mikoto.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" /> He is said to have descended to earth on the nearby [[:en:Mount Hotakadake|Mount Hotakadake]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kamikochi - A Place of Special Importance |url=https://www.snowmonkeyresorts.com/activities/kamikochi/a-place-of-special-importance |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=SNOW MONKEY RESORTS}}</ref>

He is considered the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ofune Matsuri – A Unique Festival in Nagano, Japan! - Festivals & Events｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/au3wf7b2 |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}</ref> [[Azumi-no-isora]] is also considered an ancestral deity of them, but has a different name and myth.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora |title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions |year=1993}}</ref>

He is worshipped at {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> In the central Honden.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> It is a prominent [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]<ref name="nussbaum178">[[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA178&dq= "''Engi-shiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 178.</ref>.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> An agricultural shrine in [[:en:Azumino|Azumino]] used by Azumi people who moved inland to become farmers..<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> which also worships his father [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]].<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" />

==Content from [[:en:Azumi-no-isora]]==
{{short description|Shinto kami of the seashore}}

{{More references|date=December 2009}}

{{Expand Japanese|阿曇磯良|date=January 2021}}

{{Nihongo|'''Azumi-no-isora'''|[[:ja:阿曇磯良|阿曇磯良]]|}} is a [[shinto]] [[kami]] of the seashore. He is considered to be the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]]. He is worshiped at a number of shrines, including {{Nihongo|Mekari Shrine|和布刈神社|}} of [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[Shikaumi Shrine]] on [[:en:Shika Island|Shika Island]],<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora|title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions|year=1993}}</ref>
and {{Nihongo|Shiga Shrine|志賀神社|}} of [[:en:Tsushima, Nagasaki|Tsushima]].

Azumi-no-isora is considered a (lesser) sea deity hired as navigator to bring the emissary [[Takenouchi no Sukune]] to the Dragon King (i.e., Dragon God, [[Ryūjin]]) in the late legend regarding the loan of the [[:en:Tide jewels|Tide jewels]] to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]],{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|pp=72–73}} attested in various foundation myth documents of the [[Hachiman]] cult.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=59}}
{{Refn|group="lower-alpha"|According to a certain source, Isora offered his navigational services in "exchange for a sexual relationship" with the empress.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=77}} Though "the motif of divine union between Jingu and a sea god is relatively uncommon", the notion that Azumi-no-isora obtained sexual favors from the empress is attested in shrine-foundation myth ({{illm|jisha engi|ja|寺社縁起|lt=''jisha engi''}}) document called the {{nihongo|''Rokugō kaizan Ninmmon daibosatsu hongi''|六郷開山仁聞大菩薩本紀}}.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=69}} Other shrines of the cult do not promote this idea. Isora carries out the task "without any sexual compensation" in the ''{{illm|Hachiman gudōkun|ja|八幡愚童訓|lt=''Hachiman gudōkun''}} attributed to a priest at the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]].{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=74}}}}

{{ill|Utsushihikanasaku|ja|宇都志日金拆命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushihikanasaku|qq=}} (宇都志日金拆命) is a Japanese deity, son of [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]] and the {{ill|Oyagami|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyagami|qq=}} or tutelary deity of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/utsushihikanasaku/ | title=Utsushihikanasaku • . A History . . Of Japan . 日本歴史|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="explore-azumino-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Explore Azumino! - Hotaka Shrine |url=https://azumino-e-tabi.net/en/sightseeing/hotaka-shrine |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=Explore Azumino! |publisher=[[Japan Tourism Agency]] |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="https-2">https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001562761.pdf</ref><ref name="hikes in japan">{{Cite web |date=2020-10-01 |title=Mt. Hotaka also have deities enshrined, and these deities are as their tutelaries : JINJA-GAKU 3 {{!}} HIKES IN JAPAN |url=https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201001062231/https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-date=2020-10-01 |access-date=2023-12-06}}</ref>
[[original pages]]

He is also known by the name Hotakami no Mikoto.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" /> He is said to have descended to earth on the nearby [[:en:Mount Hotakadake|Mount Hotakadake]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kamikochi - A Place of Special Importance |url=https://www.snowmonkeyresorts.com/activities/kamikochi/a-place-of-special-importance |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=SNOW MONKEY RESORTS}}</ref>

He is considered the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ofune Matsuri – A Unique Festival in Nagano, Japan! - Festivals & Events｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/au3wf7b2 |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}</ref> [[Azumi-no-isora]] is also considered an ancestral deity of them, but has a different name and myth.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora |title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions |year=1993}}</ref>

He is worshipped at {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> In the central Honden.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> It is a prominent [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]<ref name="nussbaum178">[[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA178&dq= "''Engi-shiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 178.</ref>.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> An agricultural shrine in [[:en:Azumino|Azumino]] used by Azumi people who moved inland to become farmers..<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> which also worships his father [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]].<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" />

==Content from [[:en:Azumi-no-isora]]==
{{short description|Shinto kami of the seashore}}

{{More references|date=December 2009}}

{{Expand Japanese|阿曇磯良|date=January 2021}}

{{Nihongo|'''Azumi-no-isora'''|[[:ja:阿曇磯良|阿曇磯良]]|}} is a [[shinto]] [[kami]] of the seashore. He is considered to be the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]]. He is worshiped at a number of shrines, including {{Nihongo|Mekari Shrine|和布刈神社|}} of [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[Shikaumi Shrine]] on [[:en:Shika Island|Shika Island]],<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora|title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions|year=1993}}</ref>
and {{Nihongo|Shiga Shrine|志賀神社|}} of [[:en:Tsushima, Nagasaki|Tsushima]].

Azumi-no-isora is considered a (lesser) sea deity hired as navigator to bring the emissary [[Takenouchi no Sukune]] to the Dragon King (i.e., Dragon God, [[Ryūjin]]) in the late legend regarding the loan of the [[:en:Tide jewels|Tide jewels]] to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]],{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|pp=72–73}} attested in various foundation myth documents of the [[Hachiman]] cult.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=59}}
{{Refn|group="lower-alpha"|According to a certain source, Isora offered his navigational services in "exchange for a sexual relationship" with the empress.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=77}} Though "the motif of divine union between Jingu and a sea god is relatively uncommon", the notion that Azumi-no-isora obtained sexual favors from the empress is attested in shrine-foundation myth ({{illm|jisha engi|ja|寺社縁起|lt=''jisha engi''}}) document called the {{nihongo|''Rokugō kaizan Ninmmon daibosatsu hongi''|六郷開山仁聞大菩薩本紀}}.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=69}} Other shrines of the cult do not promote this idea. Isora carries out the task "without any sexual compensation" in the ''{{illm|Hachiman gudōkun|ja|八幡愚童訓|lt=''Hachiman gudōkun''}} attributed to a priest at the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]].{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=74}}}}

{{ill|Utsushihikanasaku|ja|宇都志日金拆命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushihikanasaku|qq=}} (宇都志日金拆命) is a Japanese deity, son of [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]] and the {{ill|Oyagami|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyagami|qq=}} or tutelary deity of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/utsushihikanasaku/ | title=Utsushihikanasaku • . A History . . Of Japan . 日本歴史|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="explore-azumino-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Explore Azumino! - Hotaka Shrine |url=https://azumino-e-tabi.net/en/sightseeing/hotaka-shrine |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=Explore Azumino! |publisher=[[Japan Tourism Agency]] |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="https-2">https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001562761.pdf</ref><ref name="hikes in japan">{{Cite web |date=2020-10-01 |title=Mt. Hotaka also have deities enshrined, and these deities are as their tutelaries : JINJA-GAKU 3 {{!}} HIKES IN JAPAN |url=https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201001062231/https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-date=2020-10-01 |access-date=2023-12-06}}</ref>
[[original pages]]

He is also known by the name Hotakami no Mikoto.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" /> He is said to have descended to earth on the nearby [[:en:Mount Hotakadake|Mount Hotakadake]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kamikochi - A Place of Special Importance |url=https://www.snowmonkeyresorts.com/activities/kamikochi/a-place-of-special-importance |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=SNOW MONKEY RESORTS}}</ref>

He is considered the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ofune Matsuri – A Unique Festival in Nagano, Japan! - Festivals & Events｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/au3wf7b2 |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}</ref> [[Azumi-no-isora]] is also considered an ancestral deity of them, but has a different name and myth.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora |title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions |year=1993}}</ref>

He is worshipped at {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> In the central Honden.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> It is a prominent [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]<ref name="nussbaum178">[[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA178&dq= "''Engi-shiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 178.</ref>.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> An agricultural shrine in [[:en:Azumino|Azumino]] used by Azumi people who moved inland to become farmers..<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> which also worships his father [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]].<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" />

==Content from [[:en:Azumi-no-isora]]==
{{short description|Shinto kami of the seashore}}

{{More references|date=December 2009}}

{{Expand Japanese|阿曇磯良|date=January 2021}}

{{Nihongo|'''Azumi-no-isora'''|[[:ja:阿曇磯良|阿曇磯良]]|}} is a [[shinto]] [[kami]] of the seashore. He is considered to be the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]]. He is worshiped at a number of shrines, including {{Nihongo|Mekari Shrine|和布刈神社|}} of [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[Shikaumi Shrine]] on [[:en:Shika Island|Shika Island]],<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora|title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions|year=1993}}</ref>
and {{Nihongo|Shiga Shrine|志賀神社|}} of [[:en:Tsushima, Nagasaki|Tsushima]].

Azumi-no-isora is considered a (lesser) sea deity hired as navigator to bring the emissary [[Takenouchi no Sukune]] to the Dragon King (i.e., Dragon God, [[Ryūjin]]) in the late legend regarding the loan of the [[:en:Tide jewels|Tide jewels]] to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]],{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|pp=72–73}} attested in various foundation myth documents of the [[Hachiman]] cult.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=59}}
{{Refn|group="lower-alpha"|According to a certain source, Isora offered his navigational services in "exchange for a sexual relationship" with the empress.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=77}} Though "the motif of divine union between Jingu and a sea god is relatively uncommon", the notion that Azumi-no-isora obtained sexual favors from the empress is attested in shrine-foundation myth ({{illm|jisha engi|ja|寺社縁起|lt=''jisha engi''}}) document called the {{nihongo|''Rokugō kaizan Ninmmon daibosatsu hongi''|六郷開山仁聞大菩薩本紀}}.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=69}} Other shrines of the cult do not promote this idea. Isora carries out the task "without any sexual compensation" in the ''{{illm|Hachiman gudōkun|ja|八幡愚童訓|lt=''Hachiman gudōkun''}} attributed to a priest at the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]].{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=74}}}}

{{ill|Utsushihikanasaku|ja|宇都志日金拆命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushihikanasaku|qq=}} (宇都志日金拆命) is a Japanese deity, son of [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]] and the {{ill|Oyagami|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyagami|qq=}} or tutelary deity of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/utsushihikanasaku/ | title=Utsushihikanasaku • . A History . . Of Japan . 日本歴史|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="explore-azumino-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Explore Azumino! - Hotaka Shrine |url=https://azumino-e-tabi.net/en/sightseeing/hotaka-shrine |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=Explore Azumino! |publisher=[[Japan Tourism Agency]] |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="https-2">https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001562761.pdf</ref><ref name="hikes in japan">{{Cite web |date=2020-10-01 |title=Mt. [[original pages]]

He is also known by the name Hotakami no Mikoto.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" /> He is said to have descended to earth on the nearby [[:en:Mount Hotakadake|Mount Hotakadake]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kamikochi - A Place of Special Importance |url=https://www.snowmonkeyresorts.com/activities/kamikochi/a-place-of-special-importance |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=SNOW MONKEY RESORTS}}</ref>

He is considered the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ofune Matsuri – A Unique Festival in Nagano, Japan! - Festivals & Events｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/au3wf7b2 |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}</ref> [[Azumi-no-isora]] is also considered an ancestral deity of them, but has a different name and myth.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora |title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions |year=1993}}</ref>

He is worshipped at {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> In the central Honden.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> It is a prominent [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]<ref name="nussbaum178">[[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA178&dq= "''Engi-shiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 178.</ref>.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> An agricultural shrine in [[:en:Azumino|Azumino]] used by Azumi people who moved inland to become farmers..<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> which also worships his father [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]].<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" />

==Content from [[:en:Azumi-no-isora]]==
{{short description|Shinto kami of the seashore}}

{{More references|date=December 2009}}

{{Expand Japanese|阿曇磯良|date=January 2021}}

{{Nihongo|'''Azumi-no-isora'''|[[:ja:阿曇磯良|阿曇磯良]]|}} is a [[shinto]] [[kami]] of the seashore. He is considered to be the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]]. He is worshiped at a number of shrines, including {{Nihongo|Mekari Shrine|和布刈神社|}} of [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[Shikaumi Shrine]] on [[:en:Shika Island|Shika Island]],<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora|title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions|year=1993}}</ref>
and {{Nihongo|Shiga Shrine|志賀神社|}} of [[:en:Tsushima, Nagasaki|Tsushima]].

Azumi-no-isora is considered a (lesser) sea deity hired as navigator to bring the emissary [[Takenouchi no Sukune]] to the Dragon King (i.e., Dragon God, [[Ryūjin]]) in the late legend regarding the loan of the [[:en:Tide jewels|Tide jewels]] to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]],{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|pp=72–73}} attested in various foundation myth documents of the [[Hachiman]] cult.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=59}}
{{Refn|group="lower-alpha"|According to a certain source, Isora offered his navigational services in "exchange for a sexual relationship" with the empress.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=77}} Though "the motif of divine union between Jingu and a sea god is relatively uncommon", the notion that Azumi-no-isora obtained sexual favors from the empress is attested in shrine-foundation myth ({{illm|jisha engi|ja|寺社縁起|lt=''jisha engi''}}) document called the {{nihongo|''Rokugō kaizan Ninmmon daibosatsu hongi''|六郷開山仁聞大菩薩本紀}}.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=69}} Other shrines of the cult do not promote this idea. Isora carries out the task "without any sexual compensation" in the ''{{illm|Hachiman gudōkun|ja|八幡愚童訓|lt=''Hachiman gudōkun''}} attributed to a priest at the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]].{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=74}}}}

{{ill|Utsushihikanasaku|ja|宇都志日金拆命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushihikanasaku|qq=}} (宇都志日金拆命) is a Japanese deity, son of [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]] and the {{ill|Oyagami|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyagami|qq=}} or tutelary deity of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/utsushihikanasaku/ | title=Utsushihikanasaku • . A History . . Of Japan . 日本歴史|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="explore-azumino-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Explore Azumino! - Hotaka Shrine |url=https://azumino-e-tabi.net/en/sightseeing/hotaka-shrine |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=Explore Azumino! |publisher=[[Japan Tourism Agency]] |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="https-2">https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001562761.pdf</ref><ref name="hikes in japan">{{Cite web |date=2020-10-01 |title=Mt. Hotaka also have deities enshrined, and these deities are as their tutelaries : JINJA-GAKU 3 {{!}} HIKES IN JAPAN |url=https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201001062231/https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-date=2020-10-01 |access-date=2023-12-06}}</ref>
[[original pages]]

He is also known by the name Hotakami no Mikoto.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" /> He is said to have descended to earth on the nearby [[:en:Mount Hotakadake|Mount Hotakadake]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kamikochi - A Place of Special Importance |url=https://www.snowmonkeyresorts.com/activities/kamikochi/a-place-of-special-importance |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=SNOW MONKEY RESORTS}}</ref>

He is considered the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ofune Matsuri – A Unique Festival in Nagano, Japan! - Festivals & Events｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/au3wf7b2 |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}</ref> [[Azumi-no-isora]] is also considered an ancestral deity of them, but has a different name and myth.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora |title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions |year=1993}}</ref>

He is worshipped at {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> In the central Honden.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> It is a prominent [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]<ref name="nussbaum178">[[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA178&dq= "''Engi-shiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 178.</ref>.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> An agricultural shrine in [[:en:Azumino|Azumino]] used by Azumi people who moved inland to become farmers..<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> which also worships his father [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]].<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" />

==Content from [[:en:Azumi-no-isora]]==
{{short description|Shinto kami of the seashore}}

{{More references|date=December 2009}}

{{Expand Japanese|阿曇磯良|date=January 2021}}

{{Nihongo|'''Azumi-no-isora'''|[[:ja:阿曇磯良|阿曇磯良]]|}} is a [[shinto]] [[kami]] of the seashore. He is considered to be the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]]. He is worshiped at a number of shrines, including {{Nihongo|Mekari Shrine|和布刈神社|}} of [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[Shikaumi Shrine]] on [[:en:Shika Island|Shika Island]],<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora|title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions|year=1993}}</ref>
and {{Nihongo|Shiga Shrine|志賀神社|}} of [[:en:Tsushima, Nagasaki|Tsushima]].

Azumi-no-isora is considered a (lesser) sea deity hired as navigator to bring the emissary [[Takenouchi no Sukune]] to the Dragon King (i.e., Dragon God, [[Ryūjin]]) in the late legend regarding the loan of the [[:en:Tide jewels|Tide jewels]] to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]],{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|pp=72–73}} attested in various foundation myth documents of the [[Hachiman]] cult.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=59}}
{{Refn|group="lower-alpha"|According to a certain source, Isora offered his navigational services in "exchange for a sexual relationship" with the empress.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=77}} Though "the motif of divine union between Jingu and a sea god is relatively uncommon", the notion that Azumi-no-isora obtained sexual favors from the empress is attested in shrine-foundation myth ({{illm|jisha engi|ja|寺社縁起|lt=''jisha engi''}}) document called the {{nihongo|''Rokugō kaizan Ninmmon daibosatsu hongi''|六郷開山仁聞大菩薩本紀}}.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=69}} Other shrines of the cult do not promote this idea. Isora carries out the task "without any sexual compensation" in the ''{{illm|Hachiman gudōkun|ja|八幡愚童訓|lt=''Hachiman gudōkun''}} attributed to a priest at the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]].{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=74}}}}

{{ill|Utsushihikanasaku|ja|宇都志日金拆命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushihikanasaku|qq=}} (宇都志日金拆命) is a Japanese deity, son of [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]] and the {{ill|Oyagami|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyagami|qq=}} or tutelary deity of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/utsushihikanasaku/ | title=Utsushihikanasaku • . A History . . Of Japan . 日本歴史|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="explore-azumino-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Explore Azumino! - Hotaka Shrine |url=https://azumino-e-tabi.net/en/sightseeing/hotaka-shrine |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=Explore Azumino! |publisher=[[Japan Tourism Agency]] |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="https-2">https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001562761.pdf</ref><ref name="hikes in japan">{{Cite web |date=2020-10-01 |title=Mt. Hotaka also have deities enshrined, and these deities are as their tutelaries : JINJA-GAKU 3 {{!}} HIKES IN JAPAN |url=https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201001062231/https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-date=2020-10-01 |access-date=2023-12-06}}</ref>
[[original pages]]

He is also known by the name Hotakami no Mikoto.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" /> He is said to have descended to earth on the nearby [[:en:Mount Hotakadake|Mount Hotakadake]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kamikochi - A Place of Special Importance |url=https://www.snowmonkeyresorts.com/activities/kamikochi/a-place-of-special-importance |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=SNOW MONKEY RESORTS}}</ref>

He is considered the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ofune Matsuri – A Unique Festival in Nagano, Japan! - Festivals & Events｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/au3wf7b2 |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}</ref> [[Azumi-no-isora]] is also considered an ancestral deity of them, but has a different name and myth.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora |title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions |year=1993}}</ref>

He is worshipped at {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> In the central Honden.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> It is a prominent [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]<ref name="nussbaum178">[[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA178&dq= "''Engi-shiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 178.</ref>.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> An agricultural shrine in [[:en:Azumino|Azumino]] used by Azumi people who moved inland to become farmers..<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> which also worships his father [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]].<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" />

==Content from [[:en:Azumi-no-isora]]==
{{short description|Shinto kami of the seashore}}

{{More references|date=December 2009}}

{{Expand Japanese|阿曇磯良|date=January 2021}}

{{Nihongo|'''Azumi-no-isora'''|[[:ja:阿曇磯良|阿曇磯良]]|}} is a [[shinto]] [[kami]] of the seashore. He is considered to be the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]]. He is worshiped at a number of shrines, including {{Nihongo|Mekari Shrine|和布刈神社|}} of [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[Shikaumi Shrine]] on [[:en:Shika Island|Shika Island]],<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora|title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions|year=1993}}</ref>
and {{Nihongo|Shiga Shrine|志賀神社|}} of [[:en:Tsushima, Nagasaki|Tsushima]].

Azumi-no-isora is considered a (lesser) sea deity hired as navigator to bring the emissary [[Takenouchi no Sukune]] to the Dragon King (i.e., Dragon God, [[Ryūjin]]) in the late legend regarding the loan of the [[:en:Tide jewels|Tide jewels]] to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]],{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|pp=72–73}} attested in various foundation myth documents of the [[Hachiman]] cult.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=59}}
{{Refn|group="lower-alpha"|According to a certain source, Isora offered his navigational services in "exchange for a sexual relationship" with the empress.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=77}} Though "the motif of divine union between Jingu and a sea god is relatively uncommon", the notion that Azumi-no-isora obtained sexual favors from the empress is attested in shrine-foundation myth ({{illm|jisha engi|ja|寺社縁起|lt=''jisha engi''}}) document called the {{nihongo|''Rokugō kaizan Ninmmon daibosatsu hongi''|六郷開山仁聞大菩薩本紀}}.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=69}} Other shrines of the cult do not promote this idea. Isora carries out the task "without any sexual compensation" in the ''{{illm|Hachiman gudōkun|ja|八幡愚童訓|lt=''Hachiman gudōkun''}} attributed to a priest at the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]].{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=74}}}}

He is worshipped at {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> In the central Honden.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> It is a prominent [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]<ref name="nussbaum178">[[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA178&dq= "''Engi-shiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 178.</ref>.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> An agricultural shrine in [[:en:Azumino|Azumino]] used by Azumi people who moved inland to become farmers..<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> which also worships his father [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]].<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" />

==Content from [[:en:Azumi-no-isora]]==
{{short description|Shinto kami of the seashore}}

{{More references|date=December 2009}}

{{Expand Japanese|阿曇磯良|date=January 2021}}

{{Nihongo|'''Azumi-no-isora'''|[[:ja:阿曇磯良|阿曇磯良]]|}} is a [[shinto]] [[kami]] of the seashore. He is considered to be the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]]. He is worshiped at a number of shrines, including {{Nihongo|Mekari Shrine|和布刈神社|}} of [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[Shikaumi Shrine]] on [[:en:Shika Island|Shika Island]],<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora|title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions|year=1993}}</ref>
and {{Nihongo|Shiga Shrine|志賀神社|}} of [[:en:Tsushima, Nagasaki|Tsushima]].

Azumi-no-isora is considered a (lesser) sea deity hired as navigator to bring the emissary [[Takenouchi no Sukune]] to the Dragon King (i.e., Dragon God, [[Ryūjin]]) in the late legend regarding the loan of the [[:en:Tide jewels|Tide jewels]] to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]],{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|pp=72–73}} attested in various foundation myth documents of the [[Hachiman]] cult.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=59}}
{{Refn|group="lower-alpha"|According to a certain source, Isora offered his navigational services in "exchange for a sexual relationship" with the empress.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=77}} Though "the motif of divine union between Jingu and a sea god is relatively uncommon", the notion that Azumi-no-isora obtained sexual favors from the empress is attested in shrine-foundation myth ({{illm|jisha engi|ja|寺社縁起|lt=''jisha engi''}}) document called the {{nihongo|''Rokugō kaizan Ninmmon daibosatsu hongi''|六郷開山仁聞大菩薩本紀}}.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=69}} Other shrines of the cult do not promote this idea. Isora carries out the task "without any sexual compensation" in the ''{{illm|Hachiman gudōkun|ja|八幡愚童訓|lt=''Hachiman gudōkun''}} attributed to a priest at the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]].{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=74}}}}

{{Infobox deity
| type = Shinto
| caption = {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}} a major cult center 
| image = File:Hotaka-jinja kaguraden and haiden.jpg
| father = [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]]
| mother = 
| cult_centre = {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}
| cult_center = {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}

| parents = {{ill|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187}}

{{ill|Utsushihikanasaku|ja|宇都志日金拆命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushihikanasaku|qq=}} (宇都志日金拆命) is a Japanese deity, son of [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]] and the {{ill|Oyagami|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyagami|qq=}} or tutelary deity of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/utsushihikanasaku/ | title=Utsushihikanasaku • . A History . . Of Japan . 日本歴史|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="explore-azumino-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Explore Azumino! - Hotaka Shrine |url=https://azumino-e-tabi.net/en/sightseeing/hotaka-shrine |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=Explore Azumino! |publisher=[[Japan Tourism Agency]] |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="https-2">https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001562761.pdf</ref><ref name="hikes in japan">{{Cite web |date=2020-10-01 |title=Mt. Hotaka also have deities enshrined, and these deities are as their tutelaries : JINJA-GAKU 3 {{!}} HIKES IN JAPAN |url=https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201001062231/https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-date=2020-10-01 |access-date=2023-12-06}}</ref>
[[original pages]]

He is also known by the name Hotakami no Mikoto.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" /> He is said to have descended to earth on the nearby [[:en:Mount Hotakadake|Mount Hotakadake]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kamikochi - A Place of Special Importance |url=https://www.snowmonkeyresorts.com/activities/kamikochi/a-place-of-special-importance |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=SNOW MONKEY RESORTS}}</ref>

He is considered the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ofune Matsuri – A Unique Festival in Nagano, Japan! - Festivals & Events｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/au3wf7b2 |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}</ref> [[Azumi-no-isora]] is also considered an ancestral deity of them, but has a different name and myth.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora |title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions |year=1993}}</ref>

He is worshipped at {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> In the central Honden.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> It is a prominent [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]<ref name="nussbaum178">[[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA178&dq= "''Engi-shiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 178.</ref>.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> An agricultural shrine in [[:en:Azumino|Azumino]] used by Azumi people who moved inland to become farmers..<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> which also worships his father [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]].<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" />

==Content from [[:en:Azumi-no-isora]]==
{{short description|Shinto kami of the seashore}}

{{More references|date=December 2009}}

{{Expand Japanese|阿曇磯良|date=January 2021}}

{{Nihongo|'''Azumi-no-isora'''|[[:ja:阿曇磯良|阿曇磯良]]|}} is a [[shinto]] [[kami]] of the seashore. He is considered to be the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]]. He is worshiped at a number of shrines, including {{Nihongo|Mekari Shrine|和布刈神社|}} of [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[Shikaumi Shrine]] on [[:en:Shika Island|Shika Island]],<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora|title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions|year=1993}}</ref>
and {{Nihongo|Shiga Shrine|志賀神社|}} of [[:en:Tsushima, Nagasaki|Tsushima]].

Azumi-no-isora is considered a (lesser) sea deity hired as navigator to bring the emissary [[Takenouchi no Sukune]] to the Dragon King (i.e., Dragon God, [[Ryūjin]]) in the late legend regarding the loan of the [[:en:Tide jewels|Tide jewels]] to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]],{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|pp=72–73}} attested in various foundation myth documents of the [[Hachiman]] cult.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=59}}
{{Refn|group="lower-alpha"|According to a certain source, Isora offered his navigational services in "exchange for a sexual relationship" with the empress.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=77}} Though "the motif of divine union between Jingu and a sea god is relatively uncommon", the notion that Azumi-no-isora obtained sexual favors from the empress is attested in shrine-foundation myth ({{illm|jisha engi|ja|寺社縁起|lt=''jisha engi''}}) document called the {{nihongo|''Rokugō kaizan Ninmmon daibosatsu hongi''|六郷開山仁聞大菩薩本紀}}.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=69}} Other shrines of the cult do not promote this idea. Isora carries out the task "without any sexual compensation" in the ''{{illm|Hachiman gudōkun|ja|八幡愚童訓|lt=''Hachiman gudōkun''}} attributed to a priest at the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]].{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=74}}}}

| mother =

{{ill|Utsushihikanasaku|ja|宇都志日金拆命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushihikanasaku|qq=}} (宇都志日金拆命) is a Japanese deity, son of [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]] and the {{ill|Oyagami|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyagami|qq=}} or tutelary deity of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/utsushihikanasaku/ | title=Utsushihikanasaku • . A History . . Of Japan . 日本歴史|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="explore-azumino-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Explore Azumino! - Hotaka Shrine |url=https://azumino-e-tabi.net/en/sightseeing/hotaka-shrine |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=Explore Azumino! |publisher=[[Japan Tourism Agency]] |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="https-2">https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001562761.pdf</ref><ref name="hikes in japan">{{Cite web |date=2020-10-01 |title=Mt. Hotaka also have deities enshrined, and these deities are as their tutelaries : JINJA-GAKU 3 {{!}} HIKES IN JAPAN |url=https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201001062231/https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-date=2020-10-01 |access-date=2023-12-06}}</ref>
[[original pages]]

He is also known by the name Hotakami no Mikoto.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" /> He is said to have descended to earth on the nearby [[:en:Mount Hotakadake|Mount Hotakadake]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kamikochi - A Place of Special Importance |url=https://www.snowmonkeyresorts.com/activities/kamikochi/a-place-of-special-importance |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=SNOW MONKEY RESORTS}}</ref>

He is considered the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ofune Matsuri – A Unique Festival in Nagano, Japan! - Festivals & Events｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/au3wf7b2 |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}</ref> [[Azumi-no-isora]] is also considered an ancestral deity of them, but has a different name and myth.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora |title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions |year=1993}}</ref>

He is worshipped at {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> In the central Honden.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> It is a prominent [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]<ref name="nussbaum178">[[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA178&dq= "''Engi-shiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 178.</ref>.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> An agricultural shrine in [[:en:Azumino|Azumino]] used by Azumi people who moved inland to become farmers..<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> which also worships his father [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]].<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" />

==Content from [[:en:Azumi-no-isora]]==
{{short description|Shinto kami of the seashore}}

{{More references|date=December 2009}}

{{Expand Japanese|阿曇磯良|date=January 2021}}

{{Nihongo|'''Azumi-no-isora'''|[[:ja:阿曇磯良|阿曇磯良]]|}} is a [[shinto]] [[kami]] of the seashore. He is considered to be the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]]. He is worshiped at a number of shrines, including {{Nihongo|Mekari Shrine|和布刈神社|}} of [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[Shikaumi Shrine]] on [[:en:Shika Island|Shika Island]],<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora|title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions|year=1993}}</ref>
and {{Nihongo|Shiga Shrine|志賀神社|}} of [[:en:Tsushima, Nagasaki|Tsushima]].

Azumi-no-isora is considered a (lesser) sea deity hired as navigator to bring the emissary [[Takenouchi no Sukune]] to the Dragon King (i.e., Dragon God, [[Ryūjin]]) in the late legend regarding the loan of the [[:en:Tide jewels|Tide jewels]] to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]],{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|pp=72–73}} attested in various foundation myth documents of the [[Hachiman]] cult.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=59}}
{{Refn|group="lower-alpha"|According to a certain source, Isora offered his navigational services in "exchange for a sexual relationship" with the empress.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=77}} Though "the motif of divine union between Jingu and a sea god is relatively uncommon", the notion that Azumi-no-isora obtained sexual favors from the empress is attested in shrine-foundation myth ({{illm|jisha engi|ja|寺社縁起|lt=''jisha engi''}}) document called the {{nihongo|''Rokugō kaizan Ninmmon daibosatsu hongi''|六郷開山仁聞大菩薩本紀}}.{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=69}} Other shrines of the cult do not promote this idea. Isora carries out the task "without any sexual compensation" in the ''{{illm|Hachiman gudōkun|ja|八幡愚童訓|lt=''Hachiman gudōkun''}} attributed to a priest at the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachimangu]].{{sfnp|Rambelli|2018|p=74}}}}

| type = Shinto
| caption = {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}} a major cult center 
| image = File:Hotaka-jinja kaguraden and haiden.jpg
| father = {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi}}
| mother = 
| cult_centre = {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}
| cult_center = {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}
| gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}
| parents = {{ill|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187}}
| equivalent5_type = Japanese | equivalent5 = {{ill|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187}}
}}

{{ill|Utsushihikanasaku|ja|宇都志日金拆命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushihikanasaku|qq=}} (宇都志日金拆命) is a Japanese deity, son of {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi}} and the {{ill|Oyagami|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyagami|qq=}} or tutelary deity of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/utsushihikanasaku/ | title=Utsushihikanasaku • . A History . . Of Japan . 日本歴史|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="explore-azumino-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Explore Azumino! - Hotaka Shrine |url=https://azumino-e-tabi.net/en/sightseeing/hotaka-shrine |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=Explore Azumino! |publisher=[[Japan Tourism Agency]] |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="https-2">https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001562761.pdf</ref><ref name="hikes in japan">{{Cite web |date=2020-10-01 |title=Mt. Hotaka also have deities enshrined, and these deities are as their tutelaries : JINJA-GAKU 3 {{!}} HIKES IN JAPAN |url=https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201001062231/https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-date=2020-10-01 |access-date=2023-12-06}}</ref>
[[original pages]]

He is also known by the name Hotakami no Mikoto.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" /> He is said to have descended to earth on the nearby {{ill|Mount Hotakadake|en|Mount Hotakadake|WD=Q656026|lt=Mount Hotakadake}}.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kamikochi - A Place of Special Importance |url=https://www.snowmonkeyresorts.com/activities/kamikochi/a-place-of-special-importance |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=SNOW MONKEY RESORTS}}</ref>

He is considered the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ofune Matsuri – A Unique Festival in Nagano, Japan! - Festivals & Events｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/au3wf7b2 |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}</ref> [[Azumi-no-isora]] is also considered an ancestral deity of them, but has a different name and myth.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora |title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions |year=1993}}</ref>

He is worshipped at {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> In the central Honden.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> It is a prominent [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]<ref name="nussbaum178">{{ill|Louis Frédéric|en|Louis Frédéric|WD=Q726040|lt=Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric}}. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA178&dq= "''Engi-shiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 178.</ref>.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> An agricultural shrine in {{ill|Azumino|en|Azumino|WD=Q534667|lt=Azumino}} used by Azumi people who moved inland to become farmers..<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> which also worships his father {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" />

A History . . Of Japan . 日本歴史|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="explore-azumino-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Explore Azumino! - Hotaka Shrine |url=https://azumino-e-tabi.net/en/sightseeing/hotaka-shrine |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=Explore Azumino! |publisher=[[Japan Tourism Agency]] |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="https-2">https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001562761.pdf</ref><ref name="hikes in japan">{{Cite web |date=2020-10-01 |title=Mt. Hotaka also have deities enshrined, and these deities are as their tutelaries : JINJA-GAKU 3 {{!}} HIKES IN JAPAN |url=https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201001062231/https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-date=2020-10-01 |access-date=2023-12-06}}</ref>
[[original pages]]

He is also known by the name Hotakami no Mikoto.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" /> He is said to have descended to earth on the nearby {{ill|Mount Hotakadake|en|Mount Hotakadake|WD=Q656026|lt=Mount Hotakadake}}.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kamikochi - A Place of Special Importance |url=https://www.snowmonkeyresorts.com/activities/kamikochi/a-place-of-special-importance |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=SNOW MONKEY RESORTS}}</ref>

He is considered the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ofune Matsuri – A Unique Festival in Nagano, Japan! - Festivals & Events｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/au3wf7b2 |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}</ref> [[Azumi-no-isora]] is also considered an ancestral deity of them, but has a different name and myth.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora |title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions |year=1993}}</ref>

He is worshipped at {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> In the central Honden.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> It is a prominent [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]<ref name="nussbaum178">{{ill|Louis Frédéric|en|Louis Frédéric|WD=Q726040|lt=Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric}}. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA178&dq= "''Engi-shiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 178.</ref>.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> An agricultural shrine in {{ill|Azumino|en|Azumino|WD=Q534667|lt=Azumino}} used by Azumi people who moved inland to become farmers..<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> which also worships his father {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" />

{{ill|Utsushihikanasaku|ja|宇都志日金拆命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushihikanasaku|qq=}} (宇都志日金拆命) is a Japanese deity, son of {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi}} and the {{ill|Oyagami|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyagami|qq=}} or tutelary deity of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/utsushihikanasaku/ | title=Utsushihikanasaku • . A History . . Of Japan . 日本歴史|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="explore-azumino-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Explore Azumino! - Hotaka Shrine |url=https://azumino-e-tabi.net/en/sightseeing/hotaka-shrine |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=Explore Azumino! |publisher=[[Japan Tourism Agency]] |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="https-2">https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001562761.pdf</ref><ref name="hikes in japan">{{Cite web |date=2020-10-01 |title=Mt. Hotaka also have deities enshrined, and these deities are as their tutelaries : JINJA-GAKU 3 {{!}} HIKES IN JAPAN |url=https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201001062231/https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-date=2020-10-01 |access-date=2023-12-06}}</ref>
[[original pages]]

He is also known by the name Hotakami no Mikoto.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" /> He is said to have descended to earth on the nearby {{ill|Mount Hotakadake|en|Mount Hotakadake|WD=Q656026|lt=Mount Hotakadake}}.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kamikochi - A Place of Special Importance |url=https://www.snowmonkeyresorts.com/activities/kamikochi/a-place-of-special-importance |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=SNOW MONKEY RESORTS}}</ref>

He is considered the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ofune Matsuri – A Unique Festival in Nagano, Japan! - Festivals & Events｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/au3wf7b2 |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}</ref> [[Azumi-no-isora]] is also considered an ancestral deity of them, but has a different name and myth.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora |title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions |year=1993}}</ref>

He is worshipped at {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> In the central Honden.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> It is a prominent [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]<ref name="nussbaum178">{{ill|Louis Frédéric|en|Louis Frédéric|WD=Q726040|lt=Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric}}. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA178&dq= "''Engi-shiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 178.</ref>.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> An agricultural shrine in {{ill|Azumino|en|Azumino|WD=Q534667|lt=Azumino}} used by Azumi people who moved inland to become farmers..<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> which also worships his father {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" />

{{ill|Utsushihikanasaku|ja|宇都志日金拆命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushihikanasaku|qq=}} (宇都志日金拆命) is a Japanese deity, son of {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi}} and the {{ill|Oyagami|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyagami|qq=}} or tutelary deity of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/utsushihikanasaku/ | title=Utsushihikanasaku • . A History . . Of Japan . 日本歴史|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="explore-azumino-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Explore Azumino! - Hotaka Shrine |url=https://azumino-e-tabi.net/en/sightseeing/hotaka-shrine |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=Explore Azumino! |publisher=[[Japan Tourism Agency]] |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="https-2">https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001562761.pdf</ref><ref name="hikes in japan">{{Cite web |date=2020-10-01 |title=Mt. Hotaka also have deities enshrined, and these deities are as their tutelaries : JINJA-GAKU 3 {{!}} HIKES IN JAPAN |url=https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201001062231/https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-date=2020-10-01 |access-date=2023-12-06}}</ref>
[[original pages]]

He is also known by the name Hotakami no Mikoto.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" /> He is said to have descended to earth on the nearby {{ill|Mount Hotakadake|en|Mount Hotakadake|WD=Q656026|lt=Mount Hotakadake}}.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kamikochi - A Place of Special Importance |url=https://www.snowmonkeyresorts.com/activities/kamikochi/a-place-of-special-importance |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=SNOW MONKEY RESORTS}}</ref>

He is considered the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ofune Matsuri – A Unique Festival in Nagano, Japan! - Festivals & Events｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/au3wf7b2 |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}</ref> [[Azumi-no-isora]] is also considered an ancestral deity of them, but has a different name and myth.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora |title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions |year=1993}}</ref>

He is worshipped at {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> In the central Honden.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> It is a prominent [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]<ref name="nussbaum178">{{ill|Louis Frédéric|en|Louis Frédéric|WD=Q726040|lt=Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric}}. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA178&dq= "''Engi-shiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 178.</ref>.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> An agricultural shrine in {{ill|Azumino|en|Azumino|WD=Q534667|lt=Azumino}} used by Azumi people who moved inland to become farmers..<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> which also worships his father {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" />

{{ill|Utsushi Hikanasaku no Mikoto|ja|宇都志日金拆命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushi Hikanasaku no Mikoto|qq=}} (宇都志日金拆命) is a Japanese deity, son of {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi}} and the {{ill|Oyagami|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyagami|qq=}} or tutelary deity of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/utsushihikanasaku/ | title=Utsushi Hikanasaku no Mikoto • . A History . . Of Japan . 日本歴史|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="explore-azumino-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Explore Azumino! - Hotaka Shrine |url=https://azumino-e-tabi.net/en/sightseeing/hotaka-shrine |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=Explore Azumino! |publisher=[[Japan Tourism Agency]] |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="https-2">https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001562761.pdf</ref><ref name="hikes in japan">{{Cite web |date=2020-10-01 |title=Mt. Hotaka also have deities enshrined, and these deities are as their tutelaries : JINJA-GAKU 3 {{!}} HIKES IN JAPAN |url=https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201001062231/https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-date=2020-10-01 |access-date=2023-12-06}}</ref>
[[original pages]]

He is also known by the name Hotakami no Mikoto.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" /> He is said to have descended to earth on the nearby {{ill|Mount Hotakadake|en|Mount Hotakadake|WD=Q656026|lt=Mount Hotakadake}}.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kamikochi - A Place of Special Importance |url=https://www.snowmonkeyresorts.com/activities/kamikochi/a-place-of-special-importance |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=SNOW MONKEY RESORTS}}</ref>

He is considered the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ofune Matsuri – A Unique Festival in Nagano, Japan! - Festivals & Events｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/au3wf7b2 |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}</ref> [[Azumi-no-isora]] is also considered an ancestral deity of them, but has a different name and myth.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora |title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions |year=1993}}</ref>

He is worshipped at {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> In the central Honden.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> It is a prominent [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]<ref name="nussbaum178">{{ill|Louis Frédéric|en|Louis Frédéric|WD=Q726040|lt=Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric}}. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA178&dq= "''Engi-shiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 178.</ref>.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> An agricultural shrine in {{ill|Azumino|en|Azumino|WD=Q534667|lt=Azumino}} used by Azumi people who moved inland to become farmers..<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> which also worships his father {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" />

{{ill|Utsushi Hikanasaku no Mikoto|ja|宇都志日金拆命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushi Hikanasaku no Mikoto|qq=}} (宇都志日金拆命) is a Japanese deity, son of {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi|1=Watatsumi}} and the {{ill|Oyagami|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyagami|qq=}} or tutelary deity of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/utsushihikanasaku/ | title=Utsushi Hikanasaku no Mikoto • . A History . . Of Japan . 日本歴史|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="explore-azumino-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Explore Azumino! - Hotaka Shrine |url=https://azumino-e-tabi.net/en/sightseeing/hotaka-shrine |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=Explore Azumino! |publisher=[[Japan Tourism Agency]] |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="https-2">https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001562761.pdf</ref><ref name="hikes in japan">{{Cite web |date=2020-10-01 |title=Mt. Hotaka also have deities enshrined, and these deities are as their tutelaries : JINJA-GAKU 3 {{!}} HIKES IN JAPAN |url=https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201001062231/https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-date=2020-10-01 |access-date=2023-12-06}}</ref>
[[original pages]]

He is also known by the name Hotakami no Mikoto.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" /> He is said to have descended to earth on the nearby {{ill|Mount Hotakadake|en|Mount Hotakadake|WD=Q656026|lt=Mount Hotakadake|1=Mount Hotakadake}}.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kamikochi - A Place of Special Importance |url=https://www.snowmonkeyresorts.com/activities/kamikochi/a-place-of-special-importance |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=SNOW MONKEY RESORTS}}</ref>

He is considered the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ofune Matsuri – A Unique Festival in Nagano, Japan! - Festivals & Events｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/au3wf7b2 |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}</ref> [[Azumi-no-isora]] is also considered an ancestral deity of them, but has a different name and myth.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora |title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions |year=1993}}</ref>

He is worshipped at {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> In the central Honden.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> It is a prominent [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]<ref name="nussbaum178">{{ill|Louis Frédéric|en|Louis Frédéric|WD=Q726040|lt=Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric|1=Louis Frédéric}}. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA178&dq= "''Engi-shiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 178.</ref>.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> An agricultural shrine in {{ill|Azumino|en|Azumino|WD=Q534667|lt=Azumino|1=Azumino}} used by Azumi people who moved inland to become farmers..<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> which also worships his father {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi|1=Watatsumi}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" />

{{ill|Utsushi Hikanasaku no Mikoto|ja|宇都志日金拆命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushi Hikanasaku no Mikoto|qq=}} (宇都志日金拆命) is a Japanese deity, son of {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi|1=Watatsumi}} and the {{ill|Oyagami|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyagami|qq=}} or tutelary deity of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/utsushihikanasaku/ | title=Utsushi Hikanasaku no Mikoto • . A History . . Of Japan . 日本歴史|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="explore-azumino-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Explore Azumino! - Hotaka Shrine |url=https://azumino-e-tabi.net/en/sightseeing/hotaka-shrine |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=Explore Azumino! |publisher=[[Japan Tourism Agency]] |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="https-2">https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001562761.pdf</ref><ref name="hikes in japan">{{Cite web |date=2020-10-01 |title=Mt. Hotaka also have deities enshrined, and these deities are as their tutelaries : JINJA-GAKU 3 {{!}} HIKES IN JAPAN |url=https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201001062231/https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-date=2020-10-01 |access-date=2023-12-06}}</ref>
[[original pages]]

He is also known by the name Hotakami no Mikoto.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" /> He is said to have descended to earth on the nearby {{ill|Mount Hotakadake|en|Mount Hotakadake|WD=Q656026|lt=Mount Hotakadake|1=Mount Hotakadake}}.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kamikochi - A Place of Special Importance |url=https://www.snowmonkeyresorts.com/activities/kamikochi/a-place-of-special-importance |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=SNOW MONKEY RESORTS}}</ref>

He is considered the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ofune Matsuri – A Unique Festival in Nagano, Japan! - Festivals & Events｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/au3wf7b2 |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}</ref> [[Azumi-no-isora]] is also considered an ancestral deity of them, but has a different name and myth.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora |title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions |year=1993}}</ref>

He is worshipped at {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> In the central Honden.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> It is a prominent [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]<ref name="nussbaum178">{{ill|Louis Frédéric|en|Louis Frédéric|WD=Q726040|lt=Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric|1=Louis Frédéric}}. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA178&dq= "''Engi-shiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 178.</ref>.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> An agricultural shrine in {{ill|Azumino|en|Azumino|WD=Q534667|lt=Azumino|1=Azumino}} used by Azumi people who moved inland to become farmers..<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> which also worships his father {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi|1=Watatsumi}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" />

{{Infobox deity
| type = Shinto
| caption = {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}} a major cult center

}}

{{ill|Utsushi Hikanasaku no Mikoto|ja|宇都志日金拆命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsushi Hikanasaku no Mikoto|qq=}} (宇都志日金拆命) is a Japanese deity, son of {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi|1=Watatsumi}} and the {{ill|Oyagami|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyagami|qq=}} or tutelary deity of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/utsushihikanasaku/ | title=Utsushi Hikanasaku no Mikoto • . A History . . Of Japan . 日本歴史|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="explore-azumino-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Explore Azumino! - Hotaka Shrine |url=https://azumino-e-tabi.net/en/sightseeing/hotaka-shrine |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=Explore Azumino! |publisher=[[Japan Tourism Agency]] |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="https-2">https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001562761.pdf</ref><ref name="hikes in japan">{{Cite web |date=2020-10-01 |title=Mt. Hotaka also have deities enshrined, and these deities are as their tutelaries : JINJA-GAKU 3 {{!}} HIKES IN JAPAN |url=https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201001062231/https://hikesinjapan.yamakei-online.com/topics/jinjagaku03.php |archive-date=2020-10-01 |access-date=2023-12-06}}</ref>
[[original pages]]

He is also known by the name Hotakami no Mikoto.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" /> He is said to have descended to earth on the nearby {{ill|Mount Hotakadake|en|Mount Hotakadake|WD=Q656026|lt=Mount Hotakadake|1=Mount Hotakadake}}.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kamikochi - A Place of Special Importance |url=https://www.snowmonkeyresorts.com/activities/kamikochi/a-place-of-special-importance |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=SNOW MONKEY RESORTS}}</ref>

He is considered the ancestor of the [[Azumi people]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ofune Matsuri – A Unique Festival in Nagano, Japan! - Festivals & Events｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/au3wf7b2 |access-date=2023-12-06 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}</ref> [[Azumi-no-isora]] is also considered an ancestral deity of them, but has a different name and myth.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a0QKAQAAMAAJ&q=Azumi-no-isora |title=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |publisher=International Institute for the Study of Religions |year=1993}}</ref>

He is worshipped at {{ill|Hotaka Shrine|simple|4=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotaka Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> In the central Honden.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> It is a prominent [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]<ref name="nussbaum178">{{ill|Louis Frédéric|en|Louis Frédéric|WD=Q726040|lt=Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric|1=Louis Frédéric}}. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA178&dq= "''Engi-shiki''"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 178.</ref>.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /> An agricultural shrine in {{ill|Azumino|en|Azumino|WD=Q534667|lt=Azumino|1=Azumino}} used by Azumi people who moved inland to become farmers..<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /> which also worships his father {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|WD=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi|1=Watatsumi}}.<ref name="explore-azumino-2023a" /><ref name="https-2" /><ref name="hikes in japan" />
